metadata.title: The Fourth Book of Sentences
metadata.author: Taio of Zaragoza
known_issues: Chunk 341 contains what appears to be a mixed Latin/English sentence ('Requisitus Adam confiteri culpam noluit, quia clearly the spirit...'), suggesting possible manuscript corruption or editorial intrusion in the original.
Multiple patristic quotations from Gregory the Great's Moralia and other works require contextual introductions
The phrase 'non habere' in chunk 161 might be a textual corruption, translated contextually
Source text contains some editorial notes and variant readings that have been standardized in translation
Some biblical references may not precisely match modern critical editions
Latin biblical citations require full expansion per audio standardization rules
Translation involves extensive theological terminology requiring careful rendering for audio clarity
Translation maintains Gregory's characteristic dense theological style while aiming for clarity
The phrase 'mentiendo suam' in chunk 335 could also be translated 'by lying about their own situation' rather than 'by lying about their own actions.'
Some passages involve complex moral psychology that may need additional glossing for modern listeners
The text contains Gregory the Great's Moralia in Job, which Taio excerpted. Some attributions and specific book/chapter references may vary slightly between manuscripts.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: LIBER QUARTUS. CAPUT PRIMUM. De quadripartita qualitate locutionis.
chunks.english: Book Four. Chapter One: The Four Qualities of Speech.
chunks.notes: Opening of Book Four, establishing taxonomy of moral discourse

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omne quod dicitur, quadripartita potest qualitate distingui: si aut mala male, aut bona bene, aut mala bene, bonaque male dicantur (Moral. lib. XXII, c. 1, n. 15).
chunks.english: Everything that is said can be distinguished by four qualities: whether evil things are spoken evilly, or good things are spoken well, or evil things are spoken well, or good things are spoken badly. This teaching comes from Gregory's Moralia, book twenty-two, chapter one.
chunks.notes: Citation from Gregory the Great's Moralia in Job

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Male enim malum dicitur, cum res perversa suadetur, sicut scriptum est: Benedic Deo, et morere (Job. II, 9).
chunks.english: Evil is spoken evilly when something perverse is urged, as Scripture says in the book of Job, chapter two, verse nine: 'Bless God and die.'
chunks.notes: Example of evil speech - Job's wife urging him to curse God

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bene bonum dicitur, cum recte recta praedicantur. Sicut Joannes ait: Agite poenitentiam, appropinquavit enim regnum coelorum (Matth. III, 2).
chunks.english: Good is spoken well when right things are rightly proclaimed. As John says in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter three, verse two: 'Repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand.'
chunks.notes: John the Baptist's preaching as exemplar of good speech

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Malum bene dicitur, quando per os dicentis idcirco vitium exprimitur, ut reprobetur, sicut Paulus ait: Feminae eorum immutaverunt naturalem usum in eum qui est contra naturam (Rom. I, 26).
chunks.english: Evil is spoken well when vice is expressed by the speaker's mouth precisely in order to condemn it, as Paul says in his letter to the Romans, chapter one, verse twenty-six: 'Their women exchanged natural relations for those contrary to nature.'
chunks.notes: Paul describing vice to condemn it, not promote it

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quo videlicet loco exsecranda quoque virorum facinora subdidit, sed honeste inhonesta narravit, ut multos ad honestatis formam inhonesta narrando revocaret.
chunks.english: In that passage he also added the detestable deeds of men, but he narrated dishonorable things honorably, so that by narrating dishonorable things he might call many back to the pattern of honor.
chunks.notes: Explains Paul's rhetorical strategy of describing vice to promote virtue

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Male bonum dicitur, cum rectum aliquid recto studio non profertur, sicut illuminato caeco Pharisaei dixisse perhibentur: Tu sis discipulus ejus (Joan. IX, 28).
chunks.english: Good is spoken badly when something right is not put forward with right intention, as the Pharisees are reported to have said to the blind man who was healed, in the Gospel of John, chapter nine, verse twenty-eight: 'You are his disciple.'
chunks.notes: Pharisees speaking truth (man was Jesus' disciple) but as curse not blessing

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod maledictionis utique dixerunt studio, non orationis voto.
chunks.english: For they certainly said this with the intention of cursing, not with the desire of prayer.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vel sicut Caiphas ait: Expedit unum mori pro populo, ut non tota gens pereat (Joan. XI, 50).
chunks.english: Or as Caiaphas said in the Gospel of John, chapter eleven, verse fifty: 'It is expedient that one man die for the people, so that the whole nation not perish.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bonum quippe, sed non bene locutus est, quia dum crudelitatem necis appetiit, redemptonis gratiam prophetavit.
chunks.english: He spoke something good, but not well, because while he sought the cruelty of death, he prophesied the grace of redemption.
chunks.notes: Theological irony - evil intention producing prophetic truth

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Divine Judgments and Their Hidden Purposes
chunks.latin: CAPUT II. De diviniis judiciis.
chunks.english: Chapter Two: Divine Judgments and Their Hidden Purposes.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum valde occulta sint divina judicia, quibus in hac vita nonnunquam bonis male sit, malis bene, tunc occultiora sunt, cum et bonis hic bene est, et malis male (Moral. lib. V, c. 1, n. 1).
chunks.english: Since divine judgments are very hidden - judgments by which in this life sometimes the good fare badly and the evil fare well - they are even more hidden when the good fare well here and the evil fare badly. This comes from Gregory's Moralia, book five, chapter one.
chunks.notes: Gregory addressing problem of theodicy and prosperity theology

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam cum bonis male est, malis bene, hoc fortasse deprehenditur, quia et boni si qua deliquerunt, hic recipiunt, ut ab aeterna plenius damnatione liberentur, et mali bona, quae pro hac vita faciunt, hic inveniunt, ut ad sola in posterum tormenta pertrahantur.
chunks.english: For when things go badly for the good and well for the evil, this is perhaps grasped: that the good, if they have sinned in any way, receive punishment here so that they may be more fully freed from eternal damnation; and the evil find here the rewards for whatever good they do in this life, so that they may be drawn to torments alone in the future.
chunks.notes: Explanation of temporal suffering as purgation for the righteous

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde et ardenti in inferno diviti dicitur: Memento, fili, quia recepisti bona in vita tua, et Lazarus similiter mala (Luc. XVI, 25).
chunks.english: Hence it is also said to the rich man burning in hell, in the Gospel of Luke, chapter sixteen, verse twenty-five: 'Remember, son, that you received good things in your lifetime, and Lazarus likewise received evil things.'
chunks.notes: Parable of Dives and Lazarus as illustration

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum bonis in praesens saeculum bene est, et malis male, incertum valde fit utrum boni idcirco bona accipiant, ut provocati ad aliquid melius crescant; an justo latentique judicio hic suorum operum remunerationem recipiant, ut a praemiis vitae sequentis inanescant.
chunks.english: When things go well for the good in the present age, and badly for the evil, it becomes very uncertain whether the good receive good things so that, being provoked, they may grow toward something better; or whether by just and hidden judgment they receive here the reward for their works, so that they become empty of the rewards of the life to come.
chunks.notes: Addresses ambiguity of prosperity - is it blessing or payment in full?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ut utrum malos idcirco adversa feriant, ut ab aeternis suppliciis corrigentia defendant; an hinc eorum poena incipiat, ut quandoque complenda eos ad ultima gehennae tormenta perducat.
chunks.english: And whether evils strike the wicked so that correction may defend them from eternal punishments; or whether their punishment begins here so that when it is to be completed it may lead them to the final torments of hell.
chunks.notes: Parallel ambiguity regarding the wicked's suffering

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quia inter divina judicia gravi incertitudinis suae caligine humana mens premitur, sancti viri, cum sibi suppetere prospera hujus mundi conspiciunt, pavida suspicione turbantur.
chunks.english: Because the human mind is pressed down by the grave fog of its own uncertainty among divine judgments, holy men, when they see the prosperity of this world coming to them, are disturbed by fearful suspicion.
chunks.notes: Saints fear prosperity as possible sign of divine disfavor

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Timent enim, ne hic laborum suorum fructus recipiant, timent quod divina justitia latens in eis vulnus aspiciat, et exterioribus eos muneribus cumulans, ab intimis repellat.
chunks.english: For they fear lest they receive here the fruit of their labors; they fear that hidden divine justice sees a wound in them, and heaping external gifts upon them, repels them from inward gifts.
chunks.notes: Fear that prosperity is payment in full, excluding heavenly reward

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde per Psalmistam dicitur: Palpebrae ejus interrogant filios hominum (Psal. X, 5; lib. II in Ezech., homil. 5, n. 6).
chunks.english: Hence it is said through the Psalmist in Psalm ten, verse five: 'His eyelids examine the children of men.' This is cited from Gregory's commentary on Ezekiel, book two, homily five.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Palpebrae quippe Dei judicia sunt ipsius, quae aliquid nobis claudunt, aliquid aperiunt. Aperiendo namque nos interrogant, si intelligendo non extollimur. Claudendo nos interrogant, si non despicimus quae intelligere non valemus.
chunks.english: For the eyelids of God are his very judgments, which close something to us and open something. By opening they examine us, to see if we are not lifted up by understanding. By closing they examine us, to see if we do not despise what we cannot understand.
chunks.notes: Extended metaphor of divine eyelids as partial revelation

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Palpebris apertis cernimus, clausis nil videmus (Moral. lib. XXVIII, c. 4, n. 13).
chunks.english: With eyelids open we perceive; with them closed we see nothing. From Gregory's Moralia, book twenty-eight, chapter four.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid ergo, quid per palpebras Dei, nisi, ut paulo superius dictum est, ejus judicia accipimus? Quae juxta aliquid clauduntur hominibus, et juxta aliquid reserantur.
chunks.english: What then, what do we receive through the eyelids of God except, as was said a little earlier, his judgments? These are closed to human beings in one respect, and opened in another respect.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Superjecto ignorantiae nostrae velamine incomprehensibilitas divini judicii humanae mentis oculo nullatenus penetratur, sicut scriptum est: Judicia Dei velut abyssus multa (Psal. XXXV, 7; Moral. lib. XXIX, c. 30, n. 57).
chunks.english: With the veil of our ignorance cast over us, the incomprehensibility of divine judgment is in no way penetrated by the eye of the human mind, as it is written in Psalm thirty-five, verse seven: 'The judgments of God are like a great abyss.' This is from Gregory's Moralia, book twenty-nine, chapter thirty.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nemo judicia divina perscrutari appetat, cur cum alius repellitur, alius eligatur; vel cur cum alius eligitur, alius repellatur, quia attestante Paulo didicimus: Inscrutabilia sunt judicia ejus, et investigabiles viae ejus (Rom. XI, 33; Moral. lib. IX, c. 15, n. 22).
chunks.english: Let no one seek to scrutinize the divine judgments as to why, when one person is rejected, another is chosen; or why, when one is chosen, another is rejected. For as Paul attests, we have learned in his letter to the Romans, chapter eleven, verse thirty-three: 'His judgments are inscrutable, and his ways unsearchable.' From Gregory's Moralia, book nine, chapter fifteen.
chunks.notes: Warning against presumptuous inquiry into predestination

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Divinorum facta judiciorum semper indiscussa veneranda sunt, quia injusta esse nequaquam possunt.
chunks.english: The deeds of divine judgments are always to be revered without debate, because they can in no way be unjust.
chunks.notes: Assertion of divine justice as axiomatic

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Rationem quippe de occulto ejus consilio quaerere, nihil est aliud quam contra ejus consilium superbire.
chunks.english: Indeed, to seek the reason for his hidden counsel is nothing other than to be proud against his counsel.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum judiciorum Dei causa non deprehenditur, restat ut sub factis illius cum humilitate taceatur, quia nequaquam sufficit sensus carnis, ut secreta penetret majestatis.
chunks.english: When the cause of God's judgments is not grasped, it remains that we keep silent with humility beneath his deeds, because the sense of the flesh in no way suffices to penetrate the secrets of majesty.
chunks.notes: Silence as proper theological response to mystery

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad semetipsam humana mens redeat, et quod de divinis judiciis apprehendere non valet, non requirat, ne si divinae irae causa discutitur amplius discussa provocetur, et quam placare humilitas poterat, inexstinguibiliter superbia accendat.
chunks.english: Let the human mind return to itself, and let it not seek what it cannot grasp about divine judgments, lest if the cause of divine wrath is further examined, the wrath be provoked by the examination itself, and what humility could have placated, pride may inflame inextinguishably.
chunks.notes: Warning that theological speculation can itself be sin

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per apostolum Paulum dicitur: O homo, tu quis es, qui respondeas Deo (Rom. IX, 20; Moral. IX, c. 14, n. 21)? Semetipsum homo considerans tacet, et divina judicia discutiere metuit, qui esse se pulverem agnoscit.
chunks.english: Through the apostle Paul it is said in his letter to the Romans, chapter nine, verse twenty: 'O man, who are you to answer back to God?' From Gregory's Moralia, book nine, chapter fourteen. Considering himself, man is silent and fears to discuss divine judgments, for he recognizes himself to be dust.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Respondere Deo non posse convincitur, qui homo nominatur. Quia per hoc quod de humo sumptus est, judicia superna discutiere dignus non est.
chunks.english: He who is called 'man' is proven unable to answer back to God. Because by the fact that he was taken from the ground, he is not worthy to examine the judgments above.
chunks.notes: Etymology: homo/humus - man from earth, therefore limited

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem praedicator egregius, occulta divinorum judicia considerans, ait: O altitudo divitiarum sapientiae et scientiae Dei, quam incomprehensibilia sunt judicia ejus, et investigabiles viae ejus (Rom. XI, 35; Moral. lib. XXVIII, c. 4, n. 13)!
chunks.english: The same excellent preacher, considering the hidden divine judgments, says in his letter to the Romans, chapter eleven, verse thirty-five: 'O the depth of the riches of the wisdom and knowledge of God! How incomprehensible are his judgments, and how unsearchable his ways!' From Gregory's Moralia, book twenty-eight, chapter four.
chunks.notes: Paul's doxology as model response to mystery

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum secreta Dei judicia de repulsione Judaeorum et gentium vocatione discuteret, atque ad ea pervenire non posset, rectum valde responsum dedit, qui Deo se in ipsa ignorantia scienter inclinavit.
chunks.english: When he was examining the secret judgments of God concerning the rejection of the Jews and the calling of the Gentiles, and could not arrive at understanding them, he gave a very right response, for he knowingly inclined himself to God in that very ignorance.
chunks.notes: Learned ignorance - Paul's theological humility regarding Israel and the Church

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quantalibet quisque virtute proficiat, qualibet scientia excrescat, penetrare non sufficit, quo nos conditor moderamine judiciorum regit.
chunks.english: However much anyone may advance in virtue, however much he may grow in knowledge, he does not suffice to penetrate the governance by which the Creator rules us with the measure of his judgments.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: Rising After the Fall Into Sin
chunks.latin: CAPUT III. De his qui in peccatis labuntur, quod post ruinam surgere queant.
chunks.english: Chapter Three: Rising After the Fall Into Sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Peccata carnis experti, saltim post naufragium mare metuant, et perditionis suae discrimina vel cognita perhorrescant, ne qui pie post perpetrata mala servati sunt, haec improbe repetendo moriantur (Reg. Past. part. III, c. 28).
chunks.english: Those who have experienced the sins of the flesh should at least fear the sea after shipwreck, and should shudder at the known dangers of their own perdition, lest those who were mercifully saved after committing evils should die by wickedly repeating them. This teaching comes from Gregory's Pastoral Rule, part three, chapter twenty-eight.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Peccanti animae, et nunquam a peccato desinenti dicitur: Frons mulieris meretricis facta est tibi, noluisti erubescere (Jerem. III, 3).
chunks.english: To the soul that sins and never ceases from sin it is said in the book of the prophet Jeremiah, chapter three, verse three: 'You have the forehead of a prostitute; you refused to be ashamed.'
chunks.notes: Loss of shame as sign of hardened conscience

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi itaque sunt lapsi, ut studeant, quatenus si accepta naturae bona integra servare noluerunt, saltim scissa resarciant.
chunks.english: Therefore the fallen are to be admonished to strive so that, if they were unwilling to keep the received goods of nature intact, they may at least mend what has been torn.
chunks.notes: Second-best option: restoration if not preservation

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid dicturi sunt peccata carnis experti, si, aliis in integritate stantibus, ipsi nec post damna resipiscunt?
chunks.english: What will those who have experienced the sins of the flesh say, if others remain standing in integrity while they themselves do not come to their senses even after losses?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid dicturi sunt, si cum multi et alios secum ad regnum deferunt, hi exspectanti Domino nec semetipsos reducunt?
chunks.english: What will they say, if while many bring others with them to the kingdom, these do not even lead themselves back to the Lord who is waiting?
chunks.notes: Shame of being spiritually surpassed by others

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dicendum est lapsis, ut praeterita admissa considerent, atque imminentia devitent. Quatenus transactas culpas ad memoriam revocent, et pollui in futuris erubescant.
chunks.english: It must be said to the fallen that they should consider past wrongs committed and avoid those impending. So that they may call past faults to memory and be ashamed to be polluted in the future.
chunks.notes: Pastoral strategy: memory of sin as preventative

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Ezechielem prophetam de filiabus Judaeae dicitur: Fornicatae sunt in Aegypto in adolescentia sua; ibi subacta sunt ubera eorum, et fractae sunt mammae pubertatis earum (Ezech. XXIII, 3).
chunks.english: Through the prophet Ezekiel it is said of the daughters of Judah in chapter twenty-three, verse three: 'They played the harlot in Egypt in their youth; there their breasts were pressed and their virgin bosoms handled.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Aegypto quippe ubera subiguntur, cum turpi hujus mundi desiderio humanae mentis voluntas substernitur.
chunks.english: Indeed, breasts are pressed in Egypt when the will of the human mind is laid beneath the shameful desire of this world.
chunks.notes: Egypt as symbol of worldly bondage

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Aegypto pubertatis mammae franguntur, quando naturales sensus adhuc in semetipsis integri pulsantis concupiscentiae corruptione vitiantur.
chunks.english: In Egypt the breasts of puberty are crushed when natural senses, still intact in themselves, are corrupted by the corruption of assailing concupiscence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt peccata carnis experti, ut vigilanti cura conspiciant post delicta nobis ad se redeuntibus Deus quanta benevolentia sinum suae pietatis expandat.
chunks.english: Those who have experienced the sins of the flesh are to be admonished to see with watchful care how much benevolence God expands in the embrace of his mercy toward us when we return to him after sins.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Jeremiam prophetam Dominus dicit: Si dimiserit vir uxorem suam, et illa recedens duxerit virum alium, nunquid revertetur ad illam ultra? Nunquid non polluta et contaminata erit mulier illa? Tu autem fornicata es cum amatoribus multis; tamen revertere ad me, dicit Dominus (Jerem. III, 1).
chunks.english: Through the prophet Jeremiah the Lord says in chapter three, verse one: 'If a man divorces his wife and she departs from him and marries another man, will he return to her again? Will not that woman be utterly defiled and contaminated? But you have played the harlot with many lovers; yet return to me, says the Lord.'
chunks.notes: God's mercy exceeds human legal standards

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ecce de fornicata et relicta muliere argumentum justitiae praeponitur; et tamen nobis post lapsum redeuntibus non justitia, sed pietas exhibetur.
chunks.english: Behold, the argument of justice is set forth concerning the adulterous and abandoned woman; and yet to us returning after our fall, not justice but mercy is shown.
chunks.notes: Contrast between human and divine law

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc utique colligamus, si nobis deliquentibus tanta pietate parcitur, a nobis nec post delictum redeuntibus quanta improbitate peccatur.
chunks.english: From this we should certainly gather that if such mercy is spared for us when we sin, how much wickedness is committed by us who do not return even after sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aut quae ab illo erit improbis venia, qui non cessat vocare post culpam.
chunks.english: Or what pardon will there be from him for the wicked, who does not cease to call even after fault?
chunks.notes: God's persistence in calling magnifies refusal

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Isaias propheta dicit: Erunt oculi tui videntes praeceptorem tuum, et aures tuae audient verbum post tergum monentis (Isai. XXX, 20).
chunks.english: The prophet Isaiah says in chapter thirty, verse twenty: 'Your eyes shall see your teacher, and your ears shall hear a word behind you from one who warns.'
chunks.notes: Voice behind = God calling the backslider

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quasi enim coram positus Deo quisque verba monitionis ejus percipit, cum priusquam peccata perpetret, voluntatis ejus praecepta cognoscit.
chunks.english: For each person receives the words of his warning as if placed before God, when he knows the precepts of his will before he commits sins.
chunks.notes: Facing God = innocence; turning away = sin

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Adhuc enim ante ejus faciem stare est, necdum eum peccando contemnere. Cum vero, derelicto bono innocentiae, iniquitatem eligens appetit, terga jam in ejus faciem mittit.
chunks.english: For to still stand before his face is not yet to despise him by sinning. But when, having abandoned the good of innocence, one chooses and desires iniquity, one then turns one's back to his face.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnipotens Deus, quasi post tergum nos subsequens monet, quia etiam post culpam ad se redire persuadet.
chunks.english: Almighty God warns us as if following behind our back, because he persuades us to return to him even after fault.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aversum revocat, commissa non respicit, revertenti sinum pietatis expandit. Vocem ergo post tergum monentis audimus, si post peccata humili et contrito corde revertimur.
chunks.english: He calls back the one who has turned away, he does not regard what has been committed, he expands the embrace of mercy to the one returning. Therefore we hear the voice of the one warning behind us, if after sins we return with a humble and contrite heart.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Debemus igitur pietatem vocantis erubescere, si justitiam nolumus formidare, quia tanto graviori improbitate contemnitur, quanto et contemptus adhuc vocare non dedignatur.
chunks.english: We ought therefore to be ashamed of the mercy of the one calling, if we do not want to fear his justice, because he is scorned with all the graver wickedness when, even though scorned, he does not disdain still to call.
chunks.notes: Persistent divine calling increases human guilt

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Jeremiam prophetam Dominus dicit: Et venies usque ad Babylonem, ibi liberaberis (Mich. IV, 10; Lib. I in Ezech., homil. 10, n. 22).
chunks.english: Through the prophet Micah the Lord says in chapter four, verse ten: 'You will go as far as Babylon; there you will be delivered.' This is cited from Gregory's commentary on Ezekiel, book one, homily ten.
chunks.notes: Author attributes to Jeremiah but corrects to Micah

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Babylon quippe confusio interpretatur. Saepe enim quis postquam in confusione vitiorum ceciderit, erubescens mala quae perpetravit, ad poenitentiam redit, seque a suis lapsibus bene vivendo erigit.
chunks.english: For Babylon is interpreted as 'confusion.' Often someone, after he has fallen into the confusion of vices, blushing at the evils he has committed, returns to repentance and raises himself from his falls by living well.
chunks.notes: Babylon = confusion, but also place of repentance

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid ergo iste nisi usque ad Babylonem venit, et ibi liberatus est?
chunks.english: What then has this person done except come as far as Babylon and been freed there?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam quisque erubescens mala quae fecit, se contra se erigit, et bene operando ad statum rectitudinis redit.
chunks.english: Sometimes someone, ashamed of the evils he has done, raises himself against himself, and by acting well returns to the state of righteousness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Babylone itaque liberatus est, qui per divinam gratiam ostenditur etiam de confusione salvatus.
chunks.english: He is therefore freed in Babylon who is shown to be saved from confusion itself through divine grace.
chunks.notes: God works through the shame of sin

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Propheta ergo ad transmigrationem loquitur, cum illos increpat qui a statu rectitudinis ad erroris vitia in transmigratione ceciderunt.
chunks.english: The prophet therefore speaks to the exiles when he rebukes those who have fallen from the state of righteousness to the vices of error in exile.
chunks.notes: Exile as moral fall, also as place of redemption

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT IV. De muneribus, vel oblationibus, Deo offerendis.
chunks.english: Chapter Four: Acceptable Offerings to God.
chunks.notes: On the spirit required for worship

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ab omnipotente Deo munus ex manu non accipitur quod corde obligato in malitia profertur. Mundari etenim debet prius animus qui munus offerre vult Deo, quia omne quod datur Deo, ex dantis mente pensatur.
chunks.english: A gift is not accepted by almighty God from the hand when it is offered from a heart bound in malice. For the soul must first be cleansed if it wishes to offer a gift to God, because everything that is given to God is weighed from the mind of the giver.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnis malitiae macula ab interiore nostro homine cogitationis immutatione tergenda est, quia iram judicis placare nescit oblatio, nisi ex munditia placeat offerentis (Moral. lib. XXII, c. 14, n. 27, 28).
chunks.english: Every stain of malice must be wiped from our inner person by transformation of thought, because an offering does not know how to placate the wrath of the judge unless it pleases him from the purity of the one offering. From Gregory's Moralia, book twenty-two, chapter fourteen.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In libro Genesis scriptum est: Respexit Dominus ad Abel et ad munera ejus; ad Cain autem et ad munera illius non respexit (Gen. IV, 4, 5).
chunks.english: In the book of Genesis, chapter four, verses four and five, it is written: 'The Lord had regard for Abel and for his offering, but for Cain and for his offering he had no regard.'
chunks.notes: Paradigmatic case of accepted vs rejected offering

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Neque enim sacrum eloquium dicit: Respexit ad munera Abel, et ad Cain munera non respexit; sed prius ait quia respexit ad Abel, et deinde subjunxit, et ad munera ejus. Et rursum dicit quia non respexit ad Cain, et deinde subdidit, nec ad munera ejus.
chunks.english: For the sacred text does not say: 'He had regard for the gifts of Abel, and had no regard for the gifts of Cain'; but first it says that he had regard for Abel, and then it added, 'and for his gifts.' And again it says that he did not regard Cain, and then added, 'nor his gifts.'
chunks.notes: Careful textual analysis of Genesis word order

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex dantis namque corde id quod datur accipitur. Idcirco non Abel ex muneribus, sed ex Abel munera oblata placuerunt.
chunks.english: For what is given is received from the heart of the giver. Therefore it was not Abel from his gifts, but the gifts from Abel that pleased.
chunks.notes: Person before gift in divine evaluation

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Prius namque ad eum legitur Dominum respexisse, qui dabat, quam ad illa quae dabat. Ut audito scilicet narrationis hujus ordine discamus quia exteriora munera ex interna cordis munditia condiuntur.
chunks.english: For first the Lord is read to have regarded the one who was giving, before the things he was giving. So that having heard this order of narration we may learn that exterior gifts are seasoned by interior purity of heart.
chunks.notes: Interior purity as the 'seasoning' of gifts

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Virtus discretionis unumquemque nostrum doceat qualis apud se esse debeat, cum exteriora bona non solum Deo per oblationis votum, sed etiam proximis subministrat.
chunks.english: May the virtue of discernment teach each of us what he ought to be in himself when he provides exterior goods not only to God through the vow of offering, but also to neighbors.
chunks.notes: Application to almsgiving, not just sacrifice

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: A discordantibus accipere non vult Dominus sacrificium, holocaustum suscipere recusat. Hinc ergo perpendi debet quantum sit malum discordiae, propter quod et illud abjicitur, per quod culpa laxatur (Lib. I in Ezech., homil. 8, n. 9).
chunks.english: The Lord does not wish to accept sacrifice from those who are in discord; he refuses to receive a burnt offering. From this it must be considered how great an evil discord is, on account of which even that is rejected through which fault is loosed. From Gregory's commentary on Ezekiel, book one, homily eight.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In libro Ecclesiastico scriptum est: Qui offert sacrificium ex substantia pauperis, quasi qui victimat filium in conspectu patris sui (Eccli. XXXIV, 24; Reg. Past. part. III, c. 21).
chunks.english: In the book of Ecclesiasticus, chapter thirty-four, verse twenty-four, it is written: 'One who offers sacrifice from the substance of the poor is like one who sacrifices a son in the sight of his father.' This is cited from Gregory's Pastoral Rule, part three, chapter twenty-one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid namque esse intolerabilius potest quam mors filii ante oculos patris? Hoc itaque sacrificium quanta ira aspiciatur ostenditur, quod orbati patris dolori comparatur.
chunks.english: For what can be more intolerable than the death of a son before the eyes of his father? Thus it is shown with how much anger that sacrifice is regarded which is compared to the grief of a bereaved father.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliud est pro peccatis misericordiam facere, aliud pro misericordia facienda peccare.
chunks.english: It is one thing to do mercy for sins; it is another to sin for the sake of doing mercy.
chunks.notes: End does not justify means in almsgiving

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quae jam nec misericordia nuncupari potest, quia non ad dulcem fructum proficit, quae per virus pestiferae radicis amarescit.
chunks.english: And that cannot now even be called mercy, because it does not profit toward sweet fruit when it becomes bitter through the poison of a pestilent root.
chunks.notes: Sin-funded charity is not true charity

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Improborum hominum sacrificia per prophetam Dominus reprobat, dicens: Ego Dominus diligens judicium, et odio habens rapinam in holocausto (Isai. LXI, 8).
chunks.english: The Lord reproves the sacrifices of wicked men through the prophet, saying in the book of Isaiah, chapter sixty-one, verse eight: 'I the Lord love justice and hate robbery in burnt offering.'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerique etenim indigentibus subtrahunt quae Deo largiuntur. Sed quanta eos animadversione renuat Dominus demonstrat, dicens: Hostiae impiorum abominabiles, quae offeruntur ex scelere (Prov. XXI, 27).
chunks.english: For many indeed take from the needy what they lavish on God. But with how much censure the Lord rejects them he demonstrates, saying in the book of Proverbs, chapter twenty-one, verse twenty-seven: 'The sacrifices of the wicked are abominable, which are offered from wickedness.'
chunks.notes: Robbing poor to give to God = abomination

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque homines pessimi quanta per oblationem Deo tribuant pensant, quanta autem rapiant considerare dissimulant; quasi mercedem operum numerant et perpendere scelerum culpas recusant.
chunks.english: Very often wicked men weigh how much they give to God through offering, but pretend not to consider how much they snatch away; they count the wage of their works as it were, and refuse to weigh the faults of their crimes.
chunks.notes: Selective moral accounting among the wicked

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT V. De quinque sensibus corporis.
chunks.english: Chapter Five: The Five Bodily Senses.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Pene nullum latet quod quinque sensus corporis nostri, videlicet visus, auditus, gustus, odoratus et tactus, in omne quod sentiunt atque discernunt virtutem discretionis et sensus a cerebro trahunt (Moral. l. XI, c. 6, n. 8).
chunks.english: It is hidden from almost no one that the five senses of our body - namely sight, hearing, taste, smell, and touch - in everything they sense and discern draw the power of discernment and sensation from the brain. From Gregory's Moralia, book eleven, chapter six.
chunks.notes: Medieval neurophysiology: brain as central processor

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et cum unus sit judex sensus cerebri, qui intrinsecus praesidet, per meatus tamen proprios sensus quinque discernit.
chunks.english: And although there is one judge, the sense of the brain, which presides within, yet through proper channels it discerns the five senses.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Deus omnipotens in genere humano mirabili dispositione operatus est, ut neque oculus audiat, neque auris videat, neque os olfaciat, neque naris gustet, neque manus odorentur.
chunks.english: Almighty God has worked with wonderful arrangement in the human race, so that the eye does not hear, nor the ear see, nor the mouth smell, nor the nose taste, nor the hands perceive odors.
chunks.notes: Functional specificity of senses as divine design

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et cum uno sensu cerebri omnia disponantur, quilibet tamen horum sensus aliud facere non potest, praeter id quod ex dispositione conditoris accepit.
chunks.english: And although all things are arranged by the one sense of the brain, yet any of these senses cannot do anything other than what it has received from the arrangement of the Creator.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex istis sensibus corporalibus et exterioribus interiora et spiritualia colligenda sunt, ut per id quod in nobis publicum est transire debeamus ad secretum quod in nobis est et nosmetipsos latet.
chunks.english: From these bodily and exterior senses, interior and spiritual things are to be gathered, so that through what is public in us we should pass to what is secret in us and hidden from ourselves.
chunks.notes: Physical senses as pedagogy for spiritual understanding

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicut in Evangelio per quinque talenta quinque sensuum, id est, exteriorum, scientia exprimitur (Matth. XXV, 15; Lib. I in Evang., homil. 9, n. 1).
chunks.english: As in the Gospel, through five talents the knowledge of the five senses - that is, of exterior things - is expressed, from the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twenty-five, verse fifteen. This is from Gregory's Gospel homilies, book one, homily nine.
chunks.notes: Parable of talents = capacities including sensation

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Duobus vero intellectus et operatio demonstratur. Unius autem talenti nomine intellectus tantummodo designatur.
chunks.english: But by two talents, understanding and action are shown. By the name of one talent, understanding alone is designated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In quinque corporis sensibus unusquisque subsistit. Geminatus autem quinarius denarium perficit (Lib. I in Evang., homil. 12, n. 1).
chunks.english: Everyone exists in the five senses of the body. But the doubled five makes ten. From Gregory's Gospel homilies, book one, homily twelve.
chunks.notes: Five doubled = ten, symbolic of completeness

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quia ex utroque sexu fidelium multitudo colligitur, sancta Ecclesia decem virginibus similis denuntiatur.
chunks.english: And because the multitude of the faithful is gathered from both sexes, the holy Church is proclaimed similar to ten virgins.
chunks.notes: Ten virgins parable = whole Church, male and female

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad custodiendam cordis munditiam exteriorum sensuum nobis disciplina servanda est (Moral. lib. XXI, c. 2, n. 4).
chunks.english: To guard the purity of the heart, discipline of the exterior senses must be kept by us. From Gregory's Moralia, book twenty-one, chapter two.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam quantalibet virtute mens polleat, quantalibet gravitate vigeat, carnales tamen sensus puerile quiddam exterius praescribunt.
chunks.english: For however much virtue the mind may possess, however much seriousness it may be strong in, yet the fleshly senses prescribe something childish on the exterior.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et nisi interioris gravitatis pondere et quasi juvenili quodam vigore frenentur, ad fluxa quaeque et levia mentem enervem trahunt.
chunks.english: And unless they are reined in by the weight of interior seriousness and by a certain youthful vigor as it were, they drag the weakened mind toward things fluid and frivolous.
chunks.notes: Need for interior strength to restrain senses

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per visum plerumque frequenter in culpam labimur, si incaute quod non licet aspiciamus.
chunks.english: Through sight we very often slide into fault, if we incautiously look upon what is not permitted.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et cum sit invisibilis anima, nequaquam corporearum rerum delectatione tangitur, nisi quod inhaerens corpori, quasi quaedam egrediendi foramina ejusdem corporis sensus habet.
chunks.english: And although the soul is invisible, it is in no way touched by delight in corporeal things, except that adhering to the body, it has the senses of that body as certain openings for going out.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Jeremias propheta, de corporis sensibus narrans, ait: Ascendit mors per fenestras nostras, ingressa est domos nostras (Jerem. IX, 21).
chunks.english: The prophet Jeremiah, speaking of the senses of the body, says in chapter nine, verse twenty-one: 'Death has come up through our windows, it has entered our houses.'
chunks.notes: Windows = senses as portals for sin

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Mors quippe per fenestras ascendit, et domos ingreditur, cum per sensus corporis concupiscentia veniens habitaculum mentis irrumpit.
chunks.english: For death ascends through the windows and enters the houses when concupiscence, coming through the senses of the body, breaks into the dwelling place of the mind.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Isaiam prophetam de justis dicitur: Qui sunt hi qui ut nubes volant, et quasi columbae ad fenestras suas (Isai. LX, 8)?
chunks.english: Through the prophet Isaiah it is said of the just in chapter sixty, verse eight: 'Who are these that fly like clouds, and like doves to their windows?'

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Justi namque volare ut nubes dicti sunt, quia a terrenis contagiis sublevantur, et quasi columbae ad fenestras suas sunt, quia per sensus corporis exteriora quaeque intentione non respiciunt rapacitatis, eosque foras non rapit concupiscentia carnalis.
chunks.english: For the just are said to fly like clouds because they are lifted up from earthly contaminations, and they are like doves at their windows because through the senses of the body they do not regard exterior things with the intention of rapacity, and carnal concupiscence does not snatch them outward.
chunks.notes: Doves = gentle, innocent use of sensation

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sanctus Job, qui acceptis corporis sensibus, velut subjectis ministris, quidam aequissimus judex praeest, culpas conspicit, antequam veniant, et velut insidianti morti fenestras sui corporis claudit, dicens: Pepigi foedus cum oculis meis, ut ne cogitarem quidem de virgine (Job. XXXI, 1).
chunks.english: Holy Job, who as a most just judge presides over the received senses of the body like subject servants, sees faults before they come, and closes the windows of his body to death lying in ambush, saying in chapter thirty-one, verse one: 'I have made a covenant with my eyes not to think even about a virgin.'
chunks.notes: Job's covenant with his eyes as sensory discipline

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum sit invisibilis anima, nequaquam corporearum rerum delectatione tangitur, nisi quod inhaerens corpori, quasi quaedam egrediendi foramina ejusdem corporis sensus habet.
chunks.english: Although the soul is invisible, it is in no way touched by delight in corporeal things, except that adhering to the body, it has the senses of that body as certain openings for going out.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quinque corporis sensus, visus scilicet, auditus, gustus, odoratus et tactus, quasi quaedam viae mentis sunt quibus foras veniat, et ea que extra ejus sunt substantiam concupiscat.
chunks.english: The five senses of the body - namely sight, hearing, taste, smell, and touch - are like certain roads of the mind by which it comes outward and desires those things that are outside its substance.
chunks.notes: Senses as roads leading to external objects

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per hos etenim corporis sensus quasi per fenestras quasdam exteriora quaeque anima respicit, respiciens concupiscit.
chunks.english: For through these senses of the body, as through certain windows, the soul regards all exterior things, and regarding them, it desires them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quisquis per has corporis fenestras incaute exterius respicit, plerumque in delectationem peccati, etiam nolens, cadit; atque obligatus desideriis, incipit velle quod noluit.
chunks.english: Whoever incautiously looks outward through these windows of the body very often falls into the delight of sin even unwilling; and bound by desires, he begins to will what he did not will.
chunks.notes: Progression from careless seeing to captive willing

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeceps anima dum ante non providet, ne incaute videat quod concupiscat, caeca post incipit desiderare quod vidit.
chunks.english: The headlong soul, when it does not take care beforehand lest it incautiously see what it may desire, afterwards begins blindly to desire what it has seen.
chunks.notes: Prevention better than cure in sensory morality

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde et prophetae mens, quae sublevata saepe mysteriis internis intererat, quia alienam conjugem incaute vidit, obtenebrata postmodum sibimet illicite conjunxit (II. Reg. XI, 2).
chunks.english: Hence also the mind of the prophet, which lifted up was often present at internal mysteries, because he incautiously saw another man's wife, afterwards darkened, he unlawfully joined her to himself. This refers to the second book of Kings, chapter eleven, verse two.
chunks.notes: David and Bathsheba as cautionary example

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT VI. De Satanae tentamentis, vel fallaciis daemonum.
chunks.english: Chapter Six: Satan's Temptations and Demonic Deceptions.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Antiquus hostis bonorum vias tanta insidians arte circumvolat, ut per ea quae ab eis bene gesta cognoverit ad malitiam aditum quaerat (Moral. lib. XXXIII, c. 8, n. 15).
chunks.english: The ancient enemy flies around the paths of the good with such skill in ambush that through those things which he has learned were done well by them, he seeks an approach to malice. From Gregory's Moralia, book thirty-three, chapter eight.
chunks.notes: Satan uses our virtues against us

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam unde alium largiri quid conspicit, inde alium in discordiae flamma succendit.
chunks.english: For from the same source where he sees one person giving something generously, from there he kindles another in the flame of discord.
chunks.notes: Generosity of one used to provoke envy in another

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et cum hunc respicit misereri, illi persuadet irasci, ut dum bonum quasi non communiter factum insinuat, concordes animos a bono gratiae communis abscindat.
chunks.english: And when he sees this one showing mercy, he persuades that one to be angry, so that while he insinuates that the good was done as if not commonly, he may cut off harmonious souls from the good of common grace.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque justorum mentes antiquus hostis, cum ad mala suadendo non valet frangere, bonis satagit actibus inter eas mala seminare. Sed sancti viri has ejus insidias tanto celerius vincunt, quanto et subtilius deprehendunt.
chunks.english: Very often the ancient enemy, when he cannot break the minds of the just by persuading them to evils, strives to sow evils among them through good actions. But holy men conquer his snares all the more quickly, because they detect them all the more subtly.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XIV. De superbia et vana gloria.
chunks.english: Chapter 14: Pride and Vainglory.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Occasio perditionis nostrae facta est superbia diaboli, et argumentum redemptionis nostrae inventa est humilitas Dei (Reg. past. part. III, c. 17).
chunks.english: The devil's pride became the occasion of our ruin, and God's humility became the means of our redemption.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid elatione abjectius, quae dum supra se tenditur, ab altitudine verae celsitudinis elongatur? Initium omnis peccati superbia est (Eccli. X, 15; Moral. XXXIV, 21, n. 40).
chunks.english: What is more wretched than pride, which, while striving to rise above itself, distances itself from the height of true excellence? Pride is the beginning of all sin, as Scripture says in Ecclesiasticus, chapter ten, verse fifteen.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid est autem superbia, nisi perversae celsitudinis appetitus? Perversa enim est celsitudo, deserto eo cui debet animus inhaerere principio, sibi quodammodo fieri atque esse principium (Moral. XXVI, 17, n. 28).
chunks.english: What is pride but an appetite for perverted excellence? For that excellence is perverted when the soul, abandoning the Source to which it should cling, somehow makes itself its own beginning and end.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Multis saepe superbia luxuriae seminarium fuit, quia dum eos spiritus quasi in altum erexit, caro in infimis mersit. Qui per superbiam in secreto cordis prius elevantur, postmodum publice corruunt, quia dum occultis intumescunt motibus cordis, apertis cadunt lapsibus corporis.
chunks.english: For many, pride has often been the seedbed of lust, because while the spirit lifts them up as if to the heights, the flesh plunges them into the depths. Those who are first exalted by pride in the secret places of the heart later fall publicly, because while they swell inwardly with hidden motions of the heart, they collapse outwardly through open falls of the body.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic sic elati justa fuerant retributione feriendi, ut quia superbiendo se hominibus praeferunt, luxuriando usque ad jumentorum similitudinem devolvantur.
chunks.english: The proud deserve to be struck by this just retribution: because by their pride they set themselves above other people, they should by lust be reduced even to resemblance with beasts of burden.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Curandum nobis est et omni custodia mens a tumore servanda superbiae. Non enim ante oculos Dei vacue transvolant cogitationes nostrae, et nulla momenta temporis per animum transeunt sine statu retributionis.
chunks.english: We must take care to guard the mind with every vigilance from the swelling of pride. For our thoughts do not fly emptily before God's eyes, and no moments of time pass through the soul without a status of retribution.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Intus videt Deus quod mentem elevat, et idcirco foras permittit invalescere quod deponat. Intus prius extollitur, quod foras postmodum luxuriae corruptione feriatur.
chunks.english: God sees within what exalts the mind, and therefore he permits to grow strong outwardly that which will cast it down. What is first exalted within is later struck down outwardly by the corruption of lust.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Occultam superbiae culpam sequitur aperta percussio, ut a malis exterioribus interiora puniantur, et cor publice corruat, quod latenter tumebat.
chunks.english: Hidden guilt of pride is followed by open punishment, so that inner sins are punished by outward evils, and the heart that swelled secretly collapses publicly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Osee prophetam contra Israelitas dicitur: Spiritus fornicationis in medio eorum, et Dominum non cognoverunt (Osee V, 4). Qui ut ostenderet quod causa libidinis ex culpa proruperit elationis, mox subdidit, dicens: Et respondebit arrogantia Israel in faciem ejus (Ibid., 5).
chunks.english: Through the prophet Hosea, it is said against the Israelites, in the fifth chapter, verse four: A spirit of fornication is in their midst, and they have not known the Lord. To show that the cause of lust erupted from the fault of pride, he immediately added in verse five: And the arrogance of Israel shall testify to his face.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si auctorem suum homo superbiendo contemnit, jure et a subjecta carne praelium suscipit. Unde et ille primus inobediens, mox ut superbiendo peccavit, pudenda contexit (Gen. III, 7); quia enim contumeliam spiritus Deo intulit, mox contumeliam carnis invenit.
chunks.english: If a person through pride scorns his Creator, he rightly receives battle from his own subjected flesh. Therefore that first disobedient man, as soon as he sinned through pride, covered his shameful parts, as recounted in Genesis, chapter three, verse seven. Because he offered insult of spirit to God, he immediately discovered insult of the flesh.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et quia auctori suo esse subditus noluit, jus carnis subditae quam regebat amisit, ut in se ipso videlicet inobedientiae suae confusio redundaret, et superatus disceret quid elatus amisisset.
chunks.english: And because he refused to be subject to his Creator, he lost control over the subjected flesh that he had governed, so that the confusion of his own disobedience might overflow into himself, and being overcome, he might learn what he lost by being exalted.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum res bona agitur, necesse est ut prius ejus elatio in corde vincatur, ne si a radice miserae intentionis prodeat, amaros nequitiae fructus producat (Moral. XXIII, c. 11, n. 20).
chunks.english: When a good deed is done, it is necessary that pride be first conquered in the heart, lest if it proceeds from the root of miserable intention, it produce bitter fruits of wickedness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cordis superbia, cum exterius usque ad corpus extenditur, prius per oculos indicatur. Ipsi quippe per fastum tumoris inflati quasi e sublimi respiciunt, et quo se deprimunt, altius extollunt (Moral. XXXIV, c. 22, n. 46).
chunks.english: When pride of heart extends outwardly even to the body, it is first revealed through the eyes. For these, inflated by the disdain of swelling, look down as if from on high, and the more they debase themselves, the higher they lift themselves up.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nisi superbia per oculos se quasi per quasdam fenestras ostenderet, nequaquam Deo Psalmista dixisset: Populum humilem salvum facies, et oculos superborum humiliabis (Psal. XVII, 28).
chunks.english: Unless pride displayed itself through the eyes as through certain windows, the Psalmist would never have said to God, in Psalm seventeen, verse twenty-eight: You will save the humble people, and you will humble the eyes of the proud.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nisi superbia se per oculos funderet, Salomon quoque de Judaeae elatione non diceret: Generatio cujus excelsi sunt oculi, et palpebrae ejus in alta subrectae (Prov. XXX, 13; Reg. past. part. III, c. 17).
chunks.english: Unless pride poured itself out through the eyes, Solomon also would not have said concerning the pride of Judea, in Proverbs, chapter thirty, verse thirteen: A generation whose eyes are lofty, and whose eyelids are lifted up on high.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque elatos comitari solet liberae vocis assertio. Loquuntur quidem per superbiam elationis, et tamen loqui se credunt per libertatem rectitudinis.
chunks.english: Very often the assertion of free speech accompanies the proud. They speak through the pride of arrogance, and yet they believe they speak through the freedom of righteousness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Elatos nonnunquam ad increpanda quae non debent, aut magis increpanda quam debent, sub imagine libertatis effrenatio impellit tumoris.
chunks.english: Sometimes the proud are driven by the unbridled impulse of pride, under the guise of liberty, to rebuke what they should not, or to rebuke more than they should.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Considerandum est quod plerumque elatos utilius corripimus, si eorum correptionibus quaedam laudum fomenta misceamus.
chunks.english: We should consider that we often correct the proud more effectively if we mix some elements of praise into our corrections.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Inferenda namque illis sunt, aut alia bona quae in ipsis sunt, aut dicendum certe quae poterant esse, si non sunt; et tunc demum resecanda sunt mala quae nobis displicent, cum prius ad audiendum placabilem eorum mentem fecerint praemissa bona quae placent.
chunks.english: For we must present to them either other good qualities that are actually in them, or at least mention what could be in them if they are not there. And only then should we cut away the evils that displease us, when the good things mentioned first have made their minds receptive to listening.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam et equos indomitos blanda prius manu tangimus, ut eos nobis plenius postmodum etiam per flagella subigamus. Et amaro pigmentorum poculo mellis dulcedo adjungitur, ne ea, quae saluti profutura est, in ipso gustu aspera amaritudo sentiatur; dum vero gustus per dulcedinem fallitur, humor mortiferus per amaritudinem evacuatur.
chunks.english: For we first touch untamed horses with a gentle hand, so that we may more fully subdue them afterward even with whips. And the sweetness of honey is added to a bitter cup of medicine, lest the harsh bitterness that will benefit health be felt in the very taste. While the taste is deceived by sweetness, the deadly humor is evacuated through bitterness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In quibusdam elatis invectionis exordia permista sunt laude temperanda, ut dum admittunt favores quos diligunt, etiam correptiones recipiant quas oderunt.
chunks.english: In dealing with certain proud persons, the beginnings of rebuke must be tempered with praise, so that while they accept the favorable things they love, they may also receive the corrections they hate.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque persuadere elatis utilia melius possumus, si profectum eorum nobis potius quam illis profuturum dicamus, si eorum meliorationem nobis magis quam sibi impendi postulemus.
chunks.english: We can often better persuade the proud of useful things if we say that their progress will benefit us rather than them, if we request that their improvement be granted more for our sake than for theirs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Superbi quique eo ipso quo videri alti appetunt, a vera Dei essentia longe per elationem fiunt (Moral. XVII, c. 8, n. 10). Subsistere etenim nequeunt, quia ab aeternae essentiae soliditate dividuntur, atque hanc primam ruinam tolerant, quia per privatam gloriam in semetipsis cadunt, sicut per Psalmistam dicitur: Dejecisti eos cum allevarentur (Psal. LXXII, 18); quia eo intrinsecus corruunt, quo male extrinsecus surgunt.
chunks.english: The proud, by the very fact that they desire to appear lofty, distance themselves far from the true essence of God through pride. They cannot subsist, because they are separated from the solidity of eternal essence, and they suffer this first ruin because through private glory they fall in themselves, as it is said through the Psalmist, in Psalm seventy-two, verse eighteen: You cast them down when they were lifted up. For they collapse inwardly to the degree that they wrongly rise outwardly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic aurarum flatu in altum stipula rapitur, sed casu concito ad ima revocatur. Sic ad nubila fumus attollitur, sed repente in nihilum tumescendo dissipatur. Sic ab infimis nebula densescendo se erigit; sed exortus hanc solis radius, ac si non fuerit, abstergit.
chunks.english: Thus straw is swept up on high by the breath of wind, but by swift falling is called back to the lowest place. Thus smoke rises to the clouds, but suddenly dissipates into nothing by swelling. Thus mist rises from the lowest places by thickening; but the ray of the rising sun wipes it away as if it had never been.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sic in herbarum superficie nocturnis horis humor aspergitur, sed diurni luminis subito calore siccatur. Sic spumosae aquarum bullae inchoantibus pluviis excitatae ab intimis certatim prodeunt; sed eo celerius disruptae depereunt, quo inflatae altius extenduntur: cumque excrescunt ut appareant, crescendo peragunt ne subsistant.
chunks.english: Thus moisture is sprinkled on the surface of grass during the night hours, but is dried by the sudden heat of daylight. Thus foamy water bubbles, stirred up by beginning rains, emerge in competition from the depths; but they perish all the more quickly when burst, the higher they extend when inflated. And when they grow to become visible, by growing they accomplish their own non-existence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum quippe est: Cunctis diebus suis impius superbit (Job. XV, 20; Moral. lib. XII, c. 37, n. 42). Solent etiam electi in quibusdam suis cogitationibus atque actibus superbire; sed quia electi sunt, cunctis diebus suis superbire non possunt, quia priusquam vitam finiant, ad humilitatis metum ab elatione corda commutant.
chunks.english: For it is written, in Job, chapter fifteen, verse twenty: The wicked man is proud all his days. Even the elect are accustomed to take pride in some of their thoughts and actions. But because they are elect, they cannot be proud all their days, because before they finish life, they change their hearts from pride to the fear of humility.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unusquisque impius diebus suis omnibus superbit, quia sic vitam terminat, ut ab elatione minime recedat. Circumspicit quod temporaliter floret, et pensare negligit quod in aeternum judicatur. In vita carnis fiduciam ponit, eaque diu permanere existimat, quae ad praesens tenet.
chunks.english: Each wicked person is proud all his days, because he ends life in such a way that he scarcely departs from pride. He looks around at what flourishes temporarily, and neglects to consider what will be judged eternally. He places confidence in the life of the flesh, and believes that what he presently holds will endure for long.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Solidatur in elatione animus, in despectum adducitur omnis propinquus: quam repentina mors subrepat nunquam considerat; quam sit ejus incerta felicitas, nunquam pensat.
chunks.english: The soul is solidified in pride, and every neighbor is brought into contempt. He never considers how sudden death creeps up; he never weighs how uncertain his happiness is.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Superbire quisquam minime debuisset, etiam si annorum suorum numerum certum habere potuisset, ut sciens quantum viveret, praesciret quando se ab elatione removeret (Ibid., c. 38, n. 43).
chunks.english: No one should have been proud, even if he could have had certain knowledge of the number of his years, so that knowing how long he would live, he might know in advance when to remove himself from pride.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sciendum est quia omnis superbus juxta modum proprium tyrrannidem exercet; nam quod nonnunquam alius in republica, hoc est, per acceptam dignitatis potentiam, alius in provincia, alius in civitate, alius in domo propria, atque alius per latentem nequitiam hoc exercet apud se in cogitatione sua.
chunks.english: We must know that every proud person exercises tyranny according to his own measure. For what one person sometimes does in the state, that is, through received power of office, another does in the province, another in the city, another in his own household, and yet another exercises this through hidden wickedness within himself in his own thoughts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque superbus iram superni Judicis perpendit praesentem; sed a malo non avertitur, ut etiam ipsa quoque ab ejus interitu valeat averti (Ibid., c. 41, n. 46). Accusante se conscientia, feriri metuit; sed tamen semper auget, quo feriatur.
chunks.english: Often the proud person weighs the present anger of the Supreme Judge, but does not turn away from evil, so that even that anger itself might be able to turn away from his destruction. With conscience accusing him, he fears being struck; but nevertheless he always increases that for which he will be struck.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Contemnit quisque superbus reditum suum, desperat veniam, superbit in culpa; sed tamen testem suae nequitiae intus habet timorem. Et quamvis prava videatur foras audacter agere, de his tamen apud semetipsum cogitur trepidare.
chunks.english: Each proud person scorns his own return, despairs of pardon, and takes pride in guilt. Yet he has fear within as a witness to his wickedness. And although he seems to act boldly outwardly in evil things, he is nevertheless forced to tremble about these things within himself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Isaiam prophetam dicitur: Inebriatus est in coelo gladius meus (Isai. XXXIV, 5; Moral. lib. XXXII, c. 23, n. 49). Ac si aperte diceret: Qua ira feriam superbos terrae, perpendite, si ipsos etiam quos in coelo juxta me condidi pro elationis vitio percutere non peperci.
chunks.english: Through the prophet Isaiah, it is said in chapter thirty-four, verse five: My sword is drunk in heaven. As if he were saying openly: Consider with what anger I shall strike the proud of earth, if I did not spare from striking even those whom I created in heaven near me, on account of the vice of pride.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XV. De avaritia.
chunks.english: Chapter 15: Avarice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Avaritia, quae est idolorum servitus (Ephes. V, 5), velut amphora os cordis in ambitu apertum tenet (Moral. lib. XIV, c. 53, n. 63).
chunks.english: Avarice, which is servitude to idols, as Paul says in his letter to the Ephesians, chapter five, verse five, holds the mouth of the heart open all around like a jar.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Multi sensu torpent, sed in his quae appetunt avaritiae stimulis excitantur; et qui ad bona videnda caeci sunt, excitantibus praemiis, ad peragenda mala vigilantes fiunt.
chunks.english: Many are dull in understanding, but are aroused by the goads of avarice in those things they desire. And those who are blind to seeing good things become vigilant in doing evil things when rewards stir them up.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Zachariam prophetam de qualitate avaritiae dicitur: Haec est oculus eorum in universa terra. Et ecce talentum plumbi portabatur (Zach. V, 6).
chunks.english: Through the prophet Zechariah, concerning the quality of avarice, it is said in chapter five, verse six: This is their eye throughout all the earth. And behold, a talent of lead was being carried.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid est talentum plumbi, nisi ex eadem avaritia pondus peccati? Per plumbum metallum, cujus natura gravis est ponderis, peccatum avaritiae specialiter designatur, quod mentem quam infecerit ita gravem reddit, ut ad appetenda sublimia attolli nequaquam possit.
chunks.english: What is a talent of lead, except the weight of sin from that same avarice? Through lead, a metal whose nature is of heavy weight, the sin of avarice is especially designated, which renders the mind it has infected so heavy that it can in no way be lifted up to desire sublime things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Radix omnium malorum est cupiditas (I Tim. VI, 10; Moral. XIV, c. 53, n. 65); et quia quodlibet malum per avaritiam gignitur, dignum est ut domus avaritiae in fetore construatur.
chunks.english: Greed is the root of all evils, as Paul says in his first letter to Timothy, chapter six, verse ten. And because every evil is generated through avarice, it is fitting that the house of avarice be constructed in stench.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem Zacharias propheta dixit ad angelum: Quo istae mulieres deferunt amphoram? Et dixit ad me: Ut aedificetur ei domus in terra Sennaar (Zach. V, 11).
chunks.english: The same prophet Zechariah said to the angel in chapter five, verse eleven: Where are these women carrying the jar? And he said to me: That a house may be built for it in the land of Shinar.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sennaar quippe fetor eorum dicitur, quia sicut bonus odor ex virtute est, ita e contrario fetor ex vitio cupiditatis.
chunks.english: Shinar means their stench, because just as good odor comes from virtue, so conversely stench comes from the vice of greed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sciendum est quod Sennaar latissima vallis est, in qua turris a superbientibus aedificari coeperat, quae linguarum facta diversitate destructa est. Quae scilicet turris Babylon dicta est, pro ipsa videlicet confusione mentium atque linguarum.
chunks.english: We must know that Shinar is a very broad valley, in which a tower had begun to be built by the proud, which was destroyed when the diversity of languages was made. This tower was called Babylon, precisely because of that confusion of minds and languages.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nec immerito ibi avaritiae amphora ponitur, ubi Babylon, id est confusio, aedificatur, quia dum per avaritiam et impietatem certum est omnia mala exsurgere, recte haec ipsa avaritia atque impietas in confusione perhibentur habitare.
chunks.english: And not undeservedly is the jar of avarice placed there where Babylon, that is confusion, is built, because since it is certain that all evils arise through avarice and impiety, rightly are this very avarice and impiety said to dwell in confusion.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aestu avaritiae homines accensi, eo majora de se opera humanis oculis ostendunt, quo ampliora sibi ab hominibus offerri munera appetunt.
chunks.english: People inflamed by the heat of avarice display greater works of themselves to human eyes, to the degree that they desire more abundant gifts to be offered to them by people.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In libro beati Job scriptum est: Ignis devorabit tabernacula eorum qui munera libenter accipiunt (Job. XV, 34; Moral. lib. XII, c. 54, n. 62).
chunks.english: In the book of blessed Job, it is written in chapter fifteen, verse thirty-four: Fire will devour the tents of those who willingly receive bribes.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicut enim corpus in tabernaculo, sic mens habitat in cogitatione; sed ignis tabernacula devorat, cum aestus avaritiae cogitationes devastat.
chunks.english: For just as the body dwells in a tent, so the mind dwells in thought. But fire devours tents when the heat of avarice devastates thoughts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum repleri cuncti simul opibus inardescunt avari, audiant quod scriptum est: Qui festinat ditari, non erit innocens (Prov. XXVIII, 20; Reg. past. p. III, c. 20).
chunks.english: When greedy people all burn together to be filled with riches, let them hear what is written in Proverbs, chapter twenty-eight, verse twenty: He who hastens to be enriched will not be innocent.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui augere opes ambit, vitare peccatum negligit; et more avium captus, cum escam terrenarum rerum avidus concupiscit, quo stranguletur peccati laqueo, non agnoscit.
chunks.english: He who seeks to increase wealth neglects to avoid sin. And like a captured bird, when he greedily desires the bait of earthly things, he does not recognize the noose of sin by which he will be strangled.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum plerumque praesentis mundi lucra desiderant, et quae de futuro damna patientur ignorant, audiant hoc quod scriptum est: Haereditas ad quam festinatur in principio, in novissimo benedictione carebit (Prov. XX, 21).
chunks.english: When people often desire the gains of the present world and are ignorant of what losses they will suffer in the future, let them hear what is written in Proverbs, chapter twenty, verse twenty-one: An inheritance to which one hastens in the beginning will in the end lack blessing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui in principio haereditari festinant, sortem sibi in novissimo benedictionis amputant, quia dum per avaritiae nequitiam hic multiplicari appetunt, illic ab aeterno patrimonio exhaeredes fiunt.
chunks.english: Those who hasten to inherit in the beginning cut off for themselves the lot of blessing in the end, because while they desire to multiply here through the wickedness of avarice, there they become disinherited from the eternal inheritance.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cupiditas plerumque latenter oritur in mente, sed punctiones peccatorum omnium patenter producit in opere (Moral. lib. XX, c. 10, n. 21). Quas videlicet punctiones ab hac radice surgentes statim praedicator egregius insinuat, dicens: Quam quidam appetentes erraverunt a fide, et inseruerunt se doloribus multis (I Tim. VI, 10; Reg. past. part. III, c. 20).
chunks.english: Greed often arises secretly in the mind, but openly produces the stings of all sins in deeds. The excellent preacher immediately points out these stings rising from this root, saying in his first letter to Timothy, chapter six, verse ten: Which some coveting have erred from the faith and have entangled themselves in many sorrows.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Isaias propheta, de immensitate avaritiae humanum genus redarguens, ait: Vae qui conjungitis domum ad domum, et agrum agro copulatis, usque ad terminum loci. Nunquid habitabitis soli vos in medio terrae (Isai. V, 8)?
chunks.english: The prophet Isaiah, reproaching the human race for the immensity of avarice, says in chapter five, verse eight: Woe to you who join house to house and couple field to field, even to the boundary of the place. Will you alone dwell in the midst of the earth?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ac si aperte diceret: Quousque vos extenditis, qui non habere in communi mundo consortes minime potestis? Conjunctos quidem premitis, et contra quos vos valeatis extendere, semper invenitis.
chunks.english: As if he were saying openly: How far do you extend yourselves, you who cannot in the least have partners in the common world? You oppress those joined to you, and you always find people against whom you can extend yourselves.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XVI. De iracundia.
chunks.english: Chapter 16: Anger.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum tranquillitatem mentis plerumque ira diverberat, dilaniatam quodammodo scissamque perturbat, ut sibimetipsi non congruat, ac vim intimae similitudinis amittat (Moral. lib. V, c. 45, n. 78).
chunks.english: When anger frequently shatters the tranquility of the mind, it disturbs it as if torn and split apart, so that it does not agree with itself and loses the force of inner likeness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quanta sit iracundiae culpa pensemus, per quam dum mansuetudo amittitur, supernae imaginis similitudo vitiatur. Per iram sapientia perditur, ut quid, quove ordine agendum sit, omnino nesciatur, sicut scriptum est: Ira in sinu stulti requiescit (Eccle. VII, 10), quia nimirum intelligentiae lucem subtrahit, cum mentem permovendo confundit.
chunks.english: Let us consider how great is the guilt of anger, through which, when gentleness is lost, the likeness of the divine image is corrupted. Through anger, wisdom is lost, so that what should be done and in what order is completely unknown, as it is written in Ecclesiastes, chapter seven, verse ten: Anger rests in the bosom of fools, because it certainly withdraws the light of understanding when it confuses the mind by moving it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per iram vita amittitur, et si sapientia teneri videatur, sicut scriptum est: Ira perdit etiam prudentes (Prov. XV, 1), quia scilicet confusus animus nequaquam explet, etiam si quid intelligere prudenter valet.
chunks.english: Through anger, life is lost, even if wisdom seems to be held, as it is written in Proverbs, chapter fifteen, verse one: Anger destroys even the prudent, because a confused soul in no way fulfills what it is able to understand prudently.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per iram justitia relinquitur, sicut scriptum est: Ira viri justitiam Dei non operatur (Jac. I, 20), quia dum perturba a mens judicium suae rationis exasperat, omne quod furor suggerit rectum putat.
chunks.english: Through anger, justice is abandoned, as it is written in James, chapter one, verse twenty: The anger of man does not work the justice of God, because when the disturbed mind irritates the judgment of its own reason, it considers right everything that fury suggests.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per iram gratia vitae socialis amittitur, sicut scriptum est: Noli esse assiduus cum homine iracundo, ne discas semitas ejus, et sumas scandalum animae tuae (Prov. XXII, 24, 25). Quia qui se ex humana ratione non temperat, necesse est ut bestialiter solus vivat.
chunks.english: Through anger, the grace of social life is lost, as it is written in Proverbs, chapter twenty-two, verses twenty-four and twenty-five: Do not be a companion to an angry man, lest you learn his ways and take scandal to your soul. Because he who does not temper himself by human reason must necessarily live alone like a beast.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per iram concordia rumpitur, sicut scriptum est: Vir animosus parit rixas. Et vir iracundus effodit peccata (Prov. XV, 18). Iracundus quippe peccata effodit, quia etiam malos quos incaute ad discordiam provocat pejores facit.
chunks.english: Through anger, harmony is broken, as it is written in Proverbs, chapter fifteen, verse eighteen: A passionate man stirs up strife, and an angry man digs up sins. For the angry person digs up sins, because he also makes worse even the bad people whom he recklessly provokes to discord.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per iram lux veritatis amittitur, sicut scriptum est: Sol non occidat super iracundiam vestram (Ephes, IV, 26); quia cum menti iracundia confusionis tenebras incutit, huic Deus radium suae cognitionis abscondit.
chunks.english: Through anger, the light of truth is lost, as it is written in Paul's letter to the Ephesians, chapter four, verse twenty-six: Let not the sun go down upon your anger. Because when anger strikes the darkness of confusion into the mind, God hides from it the ray of his knowledge.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per iram sancti Spiritus splendor excluditur; quo contra, juxta vetustam translationem scriptum est: Super quem requiescet Spiritus meus, nisi super humilem, et quietum, et trementem sermones meos (Isai. LXVI, 2)? Cum ergo humilem diceret, quietum protinus adjunxit. Si ergo ira quietem mentis subtrahit, suam sancto Spiritui habitationem claudit.
chunks.english: Through anger, the splendor of the Holy Spirit is excluded. On the contrary, according to the ancient translation, it is written in Isaiah, chapter sixty-six, verse two: Upon whom shall my Spirit rest, but upon the humble and quiet person who trembles at my words?
