When he spoke of the humble, he immediately added the quiet. If therefore anger removes the quiet of the mind, it closes its dwelling place to the Holy Spirit.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Spiritus sancti recessione animus per iracundiam vacuus, ad apertam mox insaniam ducitur, et usque ad superficiem ab intimo cogitationum fundamento dissipatur.
chunks.english: By the withdrawal of the Holy Spirit, the soul emptied through anger is soon led to open madness and is dissipated even to the surface from the intimate foundation of thoughts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam irae suae stimulis accensum cor palpitat, corpus tremit, lingua se praepedit, facies ignescit, exasperantur oculi, et nequaquam recognoscuntur noti (Moral. V, c. 45, n. 79).
chunks.english: For when the heart is inflamed by the goads of anger, it palpitates, the body trembles, the tongue stumbles over itself, the face burns, the eyes grow fierce, and familiar people are not recognized at all.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ore quidem clamorem format, sed sensus quid loquatur ignorat. In nullo itaque iste ab arreptitiis longe est, qui actionis suae conscius non est.
chunks.english: The mouth indeed forms a clamor, but the mind does not know what it is saying. Therefore, this person is in no way far from the demon-possessed, who is not conscious of his own action.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque fit ut usque ad manus ira prosiliat, et quo ratio longius recedit, audacior exsurgat; seque ipsum retinere animus non valet, quia factus est potestatis alienae; et eo furor membra foras in ictibus exercet, quod intus ipsam membrorum dominam mentem captivam tenet.
chunks.english: It often happens that anger leaps forth even to the hands, and the farther reason recedes, the more boldly it arises. And the soul cannot restrain itself, because it has become subject to an alien power. And fury exercises the limbs outwardly in blows, because inwardly it holds captive the very mind that is mistress of the limbs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Aliquando homo per iracundiam manus non exerit, sed in maledictionis jaculum linguam vertit. Fratris namque interitum precibus exposcit, et hoc Deum perpetrare expetit, quod ipse perversus homo facere, vel metuit, vel erubescit.
chunks.english: Sometimes a person through anger does not extend his hands, but turns his tongue into a spear of cursing. For he demands his brother's destruction through prayers, and desires God to accomplish what he himself, as a perverse person, either fears or is ashamed to do.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Fitque ut voto et voce homicidium peragat, etiam cum a laesione proximi manibus cessat. Aliquando ira perturbato animo, quasi ex judicio silentium indicit; et quo se foras per linguam non exprimit, intus deterius ignescit, ut iratus quisque collocutionem suam proximo subtrahat, et, nihil dicendo, quam sit aversus dicat.
chunks.english: And so it happens that he commits murder by vow and voice, even when he ceases by hand from harming his neighbor. Sometimes anger imposes silence on the disturbed soul as if by judgment. And by not expressing itself outwardly through the tongue, it burns worse inwardly, so that the angry person withdraws his conversation from his neighbor and, by saying nothing, declares how averse he is.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et nonnunquam haec silentii severitas per disciplinae dispensationem geritur, si tamen sollicite in intimis discretionis forma servetur. Nonnunquam dum accensus animus a consueta locutione restringitur, per accessum temporis penitus a proximi dilectione separatur; et acriores stimuli ad mentem veniunt, dum causae quae gravius exasperant oriuntur, atque in irati oculo festuca in trabem vertitur; dum ira in odium permutatur.
chunks.english: And sometimes this severity of silence is practiced through the dispensation of discipline, if nevertheless the form of discretion is carefully preserved inwardly. Sometimes when the inflamed soul is restrained from customary speech, with the passage of time it is completely separated from love of neighbor. And sharper goads come to the mind as causes arise that exasperate more gravely, and in the eye of the angry person the speck turns into a beam as anger is transformed into hatred.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque ira per silentium clausa intra mentem vehementius aestuat, et clamosas tacita voces format, verba sibi quibus exasperetur objicit, et quasi in causae examine posita durius exasperata respondet.
chunks.english: Often anger, when shut up in silence within the mind, burns more vehemently and forms silent shouting voices, hurls at itself words by which it is exasperated, and as if placed in examination of a case, responds more harshly when exasperated.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam per iracundiam perturbatus animus majorem strepitum sui silentii sentit, eumque gravius clausa irae flamma consumit.
chunks.english: Sometimes the soul disturbed by anger feels greater tumult in its own silence, and the closed flame of anger consumes it more gravely.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sciendum est quod nonnullos ira citius accendit, facilius deserit; nonnullos vero tarde quidem commovet, sed durius tenet. Alii per iracundiam accensis calamis similes, dum vocibus perstrepunt, quasi quosdam accensionis suae sonitus reddunt; citius quidem flammam faciunt, sed protinus in favillam frigescunt (Moral. V, c. 45, n. 80).
chunks.english: We must know that anger inflames some more quickly and abandons them more easily, but moves others slowly yet holds them more severely. Some people, in anger, are like burning reeds: while they resound with voices, they produce certain sounds of their own ignition. They make flame more quickly, but immediately grow cold into ashes.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque iracundi accensionem tarde suscipiunt, sed tamen accensi semel difficillus exstinguuntur; et quia se tardius in asperitatem concitant, furoris sui durius ignem servant.
chunks.english: Often angry people receive ignition slowly, but once ignited they are extinguished with the greatest difficulty. And because they rouse themselves to harshness more slowly, they preserve the fire of their fury more severely.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque homines, quod est nequius, et citius iracundiae flammas accipiunt, et tardius deponunt; nonnulli vero has et tarde suscipiunt, et citius amittunt. In quibus nimirum quatuor modis liquido lector agnoscit quia et ad tranquillitatis bonum ultimus plusquam primus appropinquat, et in malo secundum tertius superat.
chunks.english: Often people, which is worse, both receive the flames of anger more quickly and lay them down more slowly. But some both receive them slowly and lose them more quickly. In these four modes, the reader clearly recognizes that in regard to the good of tranquility, the last type approaches it more than the first, and in evil, the third surpasses the second.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XVII. Qualiter ira reprimi debeat.
chunks.english: Chapter 17: How Anger Should Be Restrained.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Duobus modis fracta possidere animum ira desuescit (Moral. lib. V, c. 45, n. 81). Primus quippe est ut mens sollicita, antequam agere quodlibet incipiat, omnes sibi quas pati potest contumelias proponat, quatenus, Redemptoris sui probra cogitans, ad adversa se praeparet.
chunks.english: Broken anger ceases to possess the soul in two ways. The first is that the careful mind, before it begins to act in any way, should place before itself all the insults it can suffer, so that, thinking on the reproaches of its Redeemer, it may prepare itself for adversities.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quae nimirum venientia tanto fortior excipit, quanto se cautius ex praescientia armavit. Qui improvidus ab adversitate deprehenditur, quasi ab hoste dormiens invenitur, eumque citius inimicus necat, quia non repugnantem perforat.
chunks.english: It receives these coming adversities all the stronger because it armed itself more carefully through foreknowledge. He who is caught unprepared by adversity is found as if sleeping by an enemy, and the enemy kills him more quickly because he pierces one who does not resist.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam qui mala imminentia per sollicitudinem praenotat, hostiles incursus quasi in insidiis vigilans exspectat; et inde ad victoriam valenter accingitur, unde nesciens deprehendi putabatur.
chunks.english: For he who foreknows impending evils through care awaits hostile attacks vigilantly as if in ambush, and from there girds himself strongly for victory from where he was thought to be caught unknowing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Solerter animus ante actionis suae primordia cuncta debet adversa meditari, ut semper haec cogitans, semper contra haec thorace patientiae munitus, et quidquid accesserit, providus superet; et quidquid non accesserit, lucrum putet.
chunks.english: The soul must carefully meditate on all adversities before the beginnings of its action, so that always thinking on these things, always fortified against them with the breastplate of patience, it may overcome with foresight whatever comes, and consider whatever does not come as gain.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Secundus servandae mansuetudinis modus est, ut cum alienos excessus aspicimus, nostra quibus in aliis excessimus delicta cogitemus. Considerata infirmitas propria mala nobis excusat aliena.
chunks.english: The second way of preserving gentleness is that when we observe the excesses of others, we should think on our own faults by which we have transgressed against others. Our own considered weakness excuses for us the evils of others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Patienter namque illatam injuriam tolerat, qui pie meminit quod fortasse adhuc habeat in quo debeat ipse tolerari. Quasi aqua ignis exstinguitur, cum, surgente furore animi, cuique sua ad mentem culpa revocatur, quia erubescit peccata non parcere, qui vel Deo vel proximo saepe se recolit parcenda peccasse.
chunks.english: For he patiently endures an injury inflicted, who piously remembers that he perhaps still has something in which he himself should be tolerated. The fire is extinguished as if by water when, as fury of soul arises, each person's own guilt is recalled to mind, because he is ashamed not to spare sins who often remembers that he sinned in ways needing to be spared by God or neighbor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Solerter sciendum est quod alia est ira quam impatientia excitat, alia quam zelus format. Illa ex vitio, haec ex virtute generatur. Si nulla ira ex virtute surgeret, divinae animadversionis impetum Phinees per gladium non placasset (Moral. V, c. 45, n. 82).
chunks.english: We must carefully know that there is one anger which impatience excites, and another which zeal forms. The former is generated from vice, the latter from virtue. If no anger arose from virtue, Phinehas would not have appeased the assault of divine punishment through the sword.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hanc iram quia Heli non habuit, motum contra se implacabilem supernae ultionis excitavit. Nam quo contra subditorum vitia tepuit, eo contra illum ira districte aeterni rectoris exarsit.
chunks.english: Because Eli did not have this anger, he stirred up the implacable motion of divine vengeance against himself. For to the degree that he was lukewarm against the vices of his subjects, to that degree did the strict anger of the eternal Ruler blaze against him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Irascimini, inquit Psalmista, et nolite peccare (Psal. IV, 5). Quod nimirum non recte intelligunt, qui irasci nos nobis tantummodo, non etiam proximis delinquentibus volunt.
chunks.english: The Psalmist says in Psalm four, verse five: Be angry and do not sin. Those who want us to be angry only with ourselves and not also with sinning neighbors certainly do not understand this correctly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si enim sic proximos ut nos amare praecipimur, restat ut sic eorum erratibus, sicut nostris vitiis, irascamur. Unde et per Salomonem dicitur: Melior est ira risu, quia per tristitiam vultus corrigitur animus delinquentis (Eccle. VII, 4).
chunks.english: For if we are commanded to love our neighbors as ourselves, it follows that we should be angry at their errors just as at our own vices. Therefore through Solomon it is also said in Ecclesiastes, chapter seven, verse four: Anger is better than laughter, because through sadness of face the mind of the offender is corrected.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem Psalmista ait: Turbatus est prae ira oculus meus (Psal. VI, 8). Ira quippe per vitium oculum mentis excaecat, ira autem per zelum turbat, quia quos saltem rectitudinis aemulatione concutitur, ea quae nisi tranquillo corde percipi non potest, contemplatio dissipatur.
chunks.english: The same Psalmist says in Psalm six, verse eight: My eye is troubled because of anger. For anger through vice blinds the eye of the mind, but anger through zeal disturbs it, because when someone is shaken at least by the emulation of righteousness, that contemplation which can only be perceived with a tranquil heart is scattered.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque zelus rectitudinis, quia inquietudine mentem agitat, ejus mox aciem obscurat, ut altiora in commotione non videat, quae bene prius tranquilla cernebat. Sed inde subtilius ad alta reducitur, unde ad tempus, ne videat, reverberatur.
chunks.english: Often the zeal of righteousness, because it agitates the mind with restlessness, immediately obscures its keenness, so that it does not see higher things in commotion which it previously perceived well when tranquil. But from there it is more subtly led back to high things from where it is temporarily beaten back so as not to see.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ipsa zeli per mansuetudinem recta aemulatio aeterna post paululum in tranquillitate mentis oculum largius aperit, quae hunc interim per commotionem claudit. Et unde mens turbatur, ne videat, inde proficit, ut ad videndum verius clarescat.
chunks.english: This right emulation of zeal through gentleness, after a little while, opens the eye more abundantly in tranquility of mind in eternal matters, which meanwhile closes it through disturbance. And from where the mind is disturbed so as not to see, from there it profits so as to shine more truly for seeing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Infirmanti oculo, cum collyrium immittitur, lux penitus negatur; sed inde eam post paululum veraciter recipit, unde hanc ad tempus salubriter amittit.
chunks.english: When salve is applied to a weak eye, light is completely denied. But from there it truly receives light after a little while, from where it temporarily and beneficially loses it.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XVIII. De invidia.
chunks.english: Chapter 18: Envy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Invidere non possumus nisi eis quos nobis in aliquo meliores putamus (Moral. lib. V, c. 46, n. 84). Parvulus ergo est qui livore occiditur, quia ipse sibi testimonium perhibet, quod ei minor sit cujus invidia torquetur.
chunks.english: We cannot envy except those whom we consider better than ourselves in some way. Therefore, he who is killed by envy is small, because he bears witness to himself that he is lesser than the one by whose envy he is tormented.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hostis callidus primo homini invidendo subripuit quia, amissa beatitudine, minorem se immortalitati illius agnovit.
chunks.english: The cunning enemy stole up on the first man by envying him because, having lost blessedness, he recognized himself as lesser than his immortality.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cain ad perpetrandum fratricidium corruit (Gen. IV, 5, 6, 7), quia, despecto suo sacrificio, praelatum sibi infremuit, cujus Deus hostiam accepit; et quem meliorem se esse exhorruit, ne utcunque esset, amputavit.
chunks.english: Cain fell to committing fratricide, as recounted in Genesis, chapter four, verses five through seven, because, his sacrifice having been despised, he raged that one was preferred to him whose offering God accepted. And he cut off the one whom he dreaded to be better than himself, lest he exist in any way.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Esau ad persecutionem fratris hac de re exarsit, quia primogenitorum benedictione perdita, quam tamen esu lenticulae ipse vendiderat, minorem se ei quem nascendo praeibat ingemuit (Gen. XXV, 34; XXVII, 41).
chunks.english: Esau blazed forth to persecution of his brother for this reason: because, having lost the blessing of the firstborn which he himself had sold for eating lentils, he groaned that he was lesser than the one whom he preceded in birth, as recounted in Genesis, chapter twenty-five, verse thirty-four, and chapter twenty-seven, verse forty-one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Joseph sanctum fratres sui Ismaelitis transeuntibus ideo vendiderunt, quia, cognito revelationis mysterio, ne eis melior fieret, ejus profectibus obviare conati sunt (Gen. XXXVII, 27, 28).
chunks.english: His brothers sold holy Joseph to passing Ishmaelites for this reason: because, the mystery of revelation having been known, lest he become better than them, they tried to obstruct his advancement, as told in Genesis, chapter thirty-seven, verses twenty-seven and twenty-eight.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saul rex David subditum lanceam intorquendo persequitur, quia quem magis quotidie augeri virtutum successibus sensit, ultra se excrescere expavit (I Reg. XVIII, 11).
chunks.english: King Saul pursued his subject David by hurling a spear, because he feared that the one whom he perceived daily growing more in the successes of virtues would grow beyond himself, as narrated in the First Book of Samuel, chapter eighteen, verse eleven.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Parvulus est qui invidia occiditur, quia nisi ipse inferior existeret, de bono alterius non doleret.
chunks.english: He who is killed by envy is small, because unless he himself were inferior, he would not grieve over the good of another.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sciendum summopere est quia quamvis per omne vitium quod perpetratur, humano cordi antiqui hostis virus infunditur, in hac tamen nequitia, tota sua viscera serpens concutit, et imprimendae malitiae pestem vomit (Moral. V, c. 46, n. 85).
chunks.english: We must know most especially that although through every vice that is committed, the poison of the ancient enemy is poured into the human heart, yet in this wickedness the serpent shakes all his entrails and vomits forth the plague of malice to be imprinted.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De quo nimirum scriptum est: Invidia diaboli mors intravit in orbem terrarum (Sap. II, 24).
chunks.english: Concerning this it is certainly written in the Book of Wisdom, chapter two, verse twenty-four: Through the envy of the devil, death entered into the world.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam cum devictum cor livoris putredo corruperit, ipsa quoque exteriora indicant quam graviter animum vesania instigat. Per invidiam color pallore afficitur, et oculi deprimuntur, mens accenditur, et membra frigescunt; fit in cogitatione rabies, in dentibus stridor: cumque in latebris cordis crescens absconditur odium, dolore caeco tenebrat conscientiam vulnus inclusum.
chunks.english: For when the rottenness of envy has corrupted the conquered heart, even the exterior things indicate how gravely madness goads the soul. Through envy, color is affected with pallor, and eyes are cast down, the mind is inflamed, and limbs grow cold. Rage comes into thought, grinding into teeth. And when growing hatred is hidden in the hiding places of the heart, the enclosed wound darkens conscience with blind pain.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nil laetum de propriis libet, quia tabescentem mentem sua poena sauciat, quam felicitas torquet aliena; quantoque extranei operis in altum fabrica ducitur, tanto fundamentum mentis lividae profundius suffoditur, ut quo alii ad meliora properant, eo ipsa deterius ruant qua ruina videlicet etiam illud destruitur, quod in aliis actibus perfecto opere surrexisse putabatur.
chunks.english: Nothing about one's own things brings joy, because his own punishment wounds the wasting mind, which is tormented by the happiness of another. And to the degree that the structure of another's work is raised on high, to that degree is the foundation of the envious mind dug deeper beneath, so that as others hasten to better things, it itself collapses worse. By which ruin even that which was thought to have arisen in other actions through completed work is destroyed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nam invidia, cum mentem tabefecerit, cuncta quae invenerit bene gesta consumit. Per sapientissimum Salomonem dicitur: Vita carnium, sanitas cordis; putredo ossium, invidia (Prov. XIV, 30).
chunks.english: For envy, when it has wasted the mind, consumes all the good deeds it has found. Through the most wise Solomon it is said in Proverbs, chapter fourteen, verse thirty: The life of the flesh is health of heart; envy is rottenness of bones.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quid enim per carnes, nisi infirma quaedam ac tenera? Et quid per ossa, nisi fortia acta signantur? Et plerumque contingit ut quidam cum vera cordis innocentia in nonnullis suis actibus infirmi videantur, quidam vero jam quaedam ante humanos oculos robusta exerceant, sed tamen erga aliorum bona intus invidiae pestilentia tabescant.
chunks.english: For what is signified by flesh except certain weak and tender things? And what by bones except strong deeds? And it often happens that some people, with true innocence of heart, seem weak in some of their actions, while others already exercise certain strong things before human eyes, yet nevertheless waste away inwardly with the pestilence of envy toward the good things of others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bene ergo dicitur: Vita carnium sanitas cordis, quia si mentis innocentia custoditur, etiam si qua foris infirma sunt, quandoque roborantur. Et recte subditur: Putredo ossium invidia, quia per livoris vitium, ante Dei oculos pereunt etiam fortia acta virtutum. Ossa quippe putrescere est quaedam etiam robusta per invidiam deperire.
chunks.english: Well therefore is it said: The life of the flesh is health of heart, because if innocence of mind is guarded, even if there are weak things outwardly, they are sometimes strengthened. And it is rightly added: Envy is rottenness of bones, because through the vice of envy, even strong acts of virtues perish before God's eyes. For bones to rot is for certain even strong things to perish through envy.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XIX. De supprimenda invidia.
chunks.english: Chapter 19: Suppressing Envy.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Difficile namque est, ut hoc alteri non invideat, quod adipisci alter exoptat, quia quidquid temporale percipitur, tanto fit minus singulis, quanto dividitur in multis; et idcirco desiderantis mentem livor excruciat, quia hoc quod appetit, aut funditus alter accipiens adimit, aut a quantitate restringit (Moral. lib. V, c. 46, n. 86).
chunks.english: For it is difficult that one not envy another what the other desires to obtain, because whatever temporal thing is received becomes so much less for individuals as it is divided among many. And therefore envy torments the mind of the desirer, because what he desires, either another receiving it completely takes it away, or restricts it from its quantity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui livoris peste plene carere desiderat, illam haereditatem diligat quam cohaeredum numerus non angustat; quae et omnibus una est, et singulis tota; quae tanto largior ostenditur, quanto ad hanc percipientium multitudo dilatatur.
chunks.english: He who desires to be fully free from the plague of envy, let him love that inheritance which the number of co-heirs does not narrow—which is both one for all and whole for individuals, which is shown all the more generous as the multitude of receivers is expanded to it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Imminutio livoris est affectus surgens internae dulcedinis; et plena mors ejus est, perfectus amor aeternitatis.
chunks.english: The lessening of envy is affection arising from inner sweetness, and its full death is perfect love of eternity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum perfecte in amore coelestis patriae homo rapitur, plene etiam in proximi dilectione sine omni invidia solidatur, quia cum nulla terrena desiderat, nihil est quod ejus erga proximum charitati contradicat.
chunks.english: When a person is perfectly seized in love of the heavenly homeland, he is also fully solidified in love of neighbor without any envy, because when he desires nothing earthly, there is nothing to contradict his charity toward neighbor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vera charitas quid est aliud quam oculus mentis carens invidia? Qui si terreni amoris pulvere tangitur, ab internae lucis mox intuitu laesus reverberatur.
chunks.english: What is true charity other than the eye of the mind lacking envy? If it is touched by the dust of earthly love, it is immediately struck back injured from the gaze of inner light.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quia parvus est qui terrena diligit, magnus qui aeterna concupiscit, potest etiam sic non inconvenienter intelligi: Parvulum occidit invidia (Job V, 2), quoniam hujus pestis languore non moritur, nisi qui adhuc in desideriis infirmatur.
chunks.english: Because he who loves earthly things is small, and he who desires eternal things is great, it can also be understood not inappropriately thus: Envy kills the little one, as Job says in chapter five, verse two, because one does not die from the languor of this plague unless he is still weak in desires.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XX. De malitia.
chunks.english: Chapter 20: Malice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Evangelio Veritas ait: Quae vultis ut faciant vobis homines, et vos facietis (Matth. VII, 12; Moral. lib. X, c. 6, n. 8); quibus duobus utriusque testamenti mandatis, per unum malitia compescitur, per aliud benignitas praerogatur, ut quod non vult malum pati quisque non faciens, cesset a nocendi opere.
chunks.english: In the Gospel, Truth says in Matthew, chapter seven, verse twelve: Whatever you wish that people would do to you, do also to them. By these two commandments of both testaments, through one malice is restrained, through the other kindness is commanded, so that by not doing the evil that one does not wish to suffer, one ceases from the work of harming.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Curandum magnopere est ut erga procaces quisque mansuetudinem longanimitatis exhibeat, ut malitiae peste languentibus gratiam benignitatis ostendat, ut discordes pace uniat, et concordes ad concupiscentiam verae pacis accingat.
chunks.english: We must take great care that each person show the gentleness of long-suffering toward the insolent, that he display the grace of kindness to those languishing with the plague of malice, that he unite the discordant in peace, and gird the harmonious toward desire for true peace.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Dominus Jesus Christus malitiosos quosque redarguens dicit: Quid vides festucam in oculo fratris tui, et trabem in oculo tuo non vides (Matth. VII, 3, Reg. past. Part. III, c. 9)?
chunks.english: The Lord Jesus Christ, reproving all malicious people, says in Matthew, chapter seven, verse three: Why do you see the speck in your brother's eye, but do not notice the beam in your own eye?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Perturbatio quippe impatientiae festuca est; malitia vero in corde, trabes in oculo. Illam namque aura tentationis agitat, hanc autem consummatio nequitiae pene immobiliter portat.
chunks.english: For the disturbance of impatience is a speck; but malice in the heart is a beam in the eye. For the breeze of temptation agitates that, but the consummation of wickedness carries this almost immovably.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Recte vero illic subjungitur: Hypocrita, ejice primum trabem de oculo tuo, et tunc videbis ejicere festucam de oculo fratris tui.
chunks.english: And rightly it is added there: Hypocrite, first cast the beam out of your own eye, and then you will see to cast the speck out of your brother's eye.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ac si dicatur menti iniquae interius dolenti, et sanctam se exterius per patientiam demonstranti: prius a te molem malitiae excute, et tunc alios de impatientiae levitate reprehende, ne dum non studes malitiam vincere, pejus tibi sit aliena prava tolerare.
chunks.english: As if it were said to the iniquitous mind suffering inwardly and demonstrating itself holy outwardly through patience: first shake off from yourself the mass of malice, and then reprove others for the levity of impatience, lest while you do not strive to conquer malice, it be worse for you to tolerate the evil things of others.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cor pessimum ex sua et non aliena malitia tabescit (Lib. X, in Ezech. homil. 2, n. 12), sicut scriptum est: Zelus apprehendit populum ineruditum, et non signis adversarios consumit (Isai. XVI, 11, secundum version. antiquam; Moral. lib. II, c. 31, n. 51).
chunks.english: The most wicked heart wastes away from its own malice and not from another's, as it is written according to the ancient version in Isaiah, chapter sixteen, verse eleven: Zeal has seized the uneducated people, and it does not consume adversaries with signs.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicut autem ignis amoris mentem erigit, ita ignis malitiae involvit, quia et sanctus Spiritus cor quod replet elevat, et ardor malitiae ad inferiora semper incurvat.
chunks.english: But just as the fire of love lifts up the mind, so the fire of malice envelops it, because the Holy Spirit elevates the heart it fills, and the burning of malice always bends it toward lower things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Malos non solum ignis per vindictam post cruciat, sed nunc etiam per livorem cremat, quia qui post puniendi sunt retributionis supplicio, nunc semetipsos afficiunt malitiae tormento.
chunks.english: Fire torments the wicked not only afterward through vengeance, but even now burns them through envy, because those who are to be punished afterward by the punishment of retribution now afflict themselves with the torment of malice.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXI. De discordia.
chunks.english: Chapter 21: Discord.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt discordes, ut certissime sciant quia, quantislibet virtutibus polleant, spirituales fieri nullatenus possunt, si uniri per concordiam proximis negligunt (Reg. Past. part. III, c. 22).
chunks.english: The discordant must be admonished to know most certainly that, however great the virtues they possess, they can in no way become spiritual if they neglect to be united with their neighbors through harmony.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad unam igitur vocationis spem nequaquam pertingitur, si non ad eam unita cum proximis mente curratur.
chunks.english: Therefore, one in no way attains to the one hope of calling if one does not run toward it with mind united with neighbors.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe nonnulli, quo quaedam specialiter dona percipiunt, eo superbiendo donum concordiae, quod majus est, amittunt, ut si fortasse carnem prae caeteris gulae refrenatione quis edomat, concordare eis quos superat abstinendo contemnat.
chunks.english: Often some people, to the degree that they receive certain special gifts, lose by pride the gift of harmony, which is greater, so that if perhaps someone subdues the flesh beyond others by restraint of gluttony, he scorns to harmonize with those he surpasses by abstaining.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui abstinentiam a concordia separat, quid admoneat Psalmista perpendat, ait enim: Laudate eum in tympano et choro (Psal. L, 4).
chunks.english: He who separates abstinence from harmony should consider what the Psalmist admonishes, for he says in Psalm fifty, verse four: Praise him with timbrel and chorus.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In tympano namque sicca et percussa pellis resonat, in choro autem voces societate concordant. Quisquis itaque corpus affligit, sed concordiam deserit, is Deum quidem laudat in tympano, sed non laudat in choro.
chunks.english: For in the timbrel, dry and beaten skin resounds, but in the chorus, voices harmonize in fellowship. Whoever therefore afflicts the body but abandons harmony praises God indeed with the timbrel, but does not praise him in chorus.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Saepe dum quosdam major scientia erigit a caeterorum societate disjungit, et quasi quo plus sapiunt, eo a concordiae virtute desipiscunt.
chunks.english: Often when greater knowledge lifts up certain people, it separates them from fellowship with others, and as if the more they know, the more they become foolish away from the virtue of harmony.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quo quisque melius sapit, eo concordiam deserens deterius delinquit; et idcirco inexcusabiliter merebitur supplicium, quia prudenter, si voluisset, potuit vitare peccatum.
chunks.english: The better someone knows, the worse he sins by abandoning harmony. And therefore he will inexcusably deserve punishment, because prudently, if he had wished, he could have avoided sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quibusdam a concordia recedentibus recte per Jacobum dicitur: Si zelum amarum habetis, et contentiones in cordibus vestris, nolite gloriari, et mendaces esse adversus veritatem. Non est ista sapientia desursum descendens, sed terrena, animalis, diabolica; quae autem desursum est sapientia primum quidem pudica est, deinde pacifica (Jac. III, 14, 15, 17).
chunks.english: To certain people departing from harmony, James rightly says in chapter three, verses fourteen, fifteen, and seventeen: If you have bitter jealousy and contentions in your hearts, do not boast and be false to the truth. This is not wisdom descending from above, but earthly, sensual, demonic. But the wisdom from above is first pure, then peaceable.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Pudica, videlicet, quia caste intelligit; pacifica autem, quia per elationem se minime a proximorum charitate discordat.
chunks.english: Pure, namely, because it understands chastely; but peaceable, because through pride it in no way discords from the charity of neighbors.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum est: Si offeras munus tuum ad altare, et ibi recordatus fueris quia frater tuus habet aliquid adversum te, relinque ibi munus tuum ante altare, et vade prius reconciliari fratri tuo, et tunc veniens offeres munus tuum (Matth. V, 28).
chunks.english: It is written in Matthew, chapter five, verse twenty-eight: If you offer your gift at the altar and there remember that your brother has something against you, leave your gift there before the altar, and go first to be reconciled to your brother, and then coming you shall offer your gift.
chunks.notes: Verse reference should be Matthew 5:23-24, but following source text as given.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex qua scilicet praeceptione pensandum est quorum hostia repellitur quam intolerabilis culpa monstratur. Cum mala cuncta bonis sequentibus diluantur, pensemus quanta sint mala discordiae, quae nisi exstincta funditus fuerint, bonum subsequi non permittunt.
chunks.english: From this precept we must consider whose sacrifice is rejected, how intolerable the fault is shown to be. Since all evils are washed away by good deeds following, let us consider how great are the evils of discord, which, unless they are completely extinguished, do not permit good to follow.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Admonendi sunt discordes, ut si aures a mandatis coelestibus declinant, mentis oculos ad considerandum ea quae in infimis versantur aperiant.
chunks.english: The discordant must be admonished that if they turn their ears away from heavenly commands, they should open the eyes of their mind to consider those things that dwell in the lowest places.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXII. De odio.
chunks.english: Chapter 22: Hatred.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si ab increpatione hominum idcirco reticemus, quia contra nos insurgere odia formidamus, non jam lucra Dei, sed nostra procul dubio quaerimus (Moral. XX, 21, c. 21, n. 47).
chunks.english: If we keep silent from reproving people because we fear that hatreds will rise against us, we doubtless seek not God's gain but our own.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sciendum magnopere est quod nonnunquam cum redarguuntur pravi, deteriores existunt, nosque magnis odiis insequuntur. Ipsis ergo, et non nobis parcimus, si ab eorum redargutione pro eorum amore cessamus.
chunks.english: We must know greatly that sometimes when the wicked are reproved, they become worse and pursue us with great hatreds. Therefore we spare them, not ourselves, if we cease from reproving them out of love for them.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Necesse nobis est ut aliquando toleremus tacendo odiosos quod sunt, quatenus in nobis discant vivendo quod non sunt.
chunks.english: It is necessary for us sometimes to tolerate by keeping silent what hateful people are, so that by living among us they may learn what they are not.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Non est omnino justo timendum, ne dum quisque corripitur, contumelias inferat, sed ne, tractus ad odium, pejor fiat (Moral. lib. VIII, c. 42, n. 67).
chunks.english: The righteous person should not at all fear that someone, when corrected, will inflict insults, but rather that, drawn to hatred, he may become worse.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cavendum summopere est ne immoderate linguam loquacitas pertrahat, eamque usque ad lasciviam obtrectationis extendat, ne odium malitiae excitet, et os usque ad jaculum maledictionis inclinet (Moral. lib. X, c. 6, n. 8).
chunks.english: We must take utmost care lest talkativeness immoderately drag the tongue and extend it even to the wantonness of slander, lest it stir up the hatred of malice and bend the mouth even to the spear of cursing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Evangelio Veritas ait: Quae vultis ut faciant vobis homines, et vos facite eis (Matth. VII, 12). Ac si aperte dicat: Quod ab alio tibi odis fieri, vide tu ne alteri per odium facias (Tob. IV, 16).
chunks.english: In the Gospel, Truth says in Matthew, chapter seven, verse twelve: Whatever you wish that people would do to you, do also to them. As if he were saying openly: What you hate another to do to you, see that you do not do to another through hatred, as it says in Tobit, chapter four, verse sixteen.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXIII. De concupiscentia oculorum.
chunks.english: Chapter 23: Lust of the Eyes.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quisquis per corporis fenestras incaute exterius respicit, plerumque in delectationem peccati etiam nolens cadit, atque obligatus desideriis incipit velle quod noluit (Moral. lib. XXI, c. 2, n. 4).
chunks.english: Whoever looks outwardly carelessly through the windows of the body often falls into the delight of sin even unwillingly, and bound by desires begins to will what he did not will.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Praeceps anima, dum ante non providet, ne incaute videat quod concupiscat, caeca post incipit desiderare quod vidit. Unde prophetae mens, quae sublevata saepe mysteriis internis intererat, qui alienam conjugem incaute vidit, obtenebrata postmodum sibimet illicite conjunxit (II Reg. XI, 2).
chunks.english: The rash soul, because it does not foresee beforehand lest it carelessly see what it might desire, afterward begins blindly to desire what it saw. Therefore the mind of the prophet, which lifted up often dwelt in inner mysteries, when he carelessly saw another's wife, afterward darkened, unlawfully joined her to himself, as told in the Second Book of Samuel, chapter eleven, verse two.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sanctus vir, quia acceptis corporis sensibus velut subjectis ministris quidam aequissimus judex praeest, culpas conspicit, antequam veniant, et velut insidianti morti fenestras corporis claudit, dicens: Pepigi foedus cum oculis meis, ut ne cogitarem quidem de virgine (Job. XXXI, 1), ne scilicet prius incaute aspicerent quod postmodum invitus amaret.
chunks.english: The holy man, because with the senses of the body received as subject servants he presides as a most just judge, perceives faults before they come and closes the windows of the body as if to death lying in ambush, saying in Job, chapter thirty-one, verse one: I have made a covenant with my eyes, that I would not even think about a virgin, lest they carelessly look first at what he would afterward love unwillingly.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Semel species formae si cordi per oculos fuerit illigata, vix magni luctaminis manu solvitur. Ne ergo quaedam lubrica in cogitatione versemus, providendum nobis est quia intueri non debet quod non licet concupisci.
chunks.english: Once the form of beauty has been bound to the heart through the eyes, it is scarcely loosened by the hand of great struggle. Therefore, lest we turn over certain slippery things in thought, we must take care that we should not gaze upon what it is not lawful to desire.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ut munda mens in cogitatione servetur, a lascivia voluptatis suae deprimendi sunt oculi, quasi quidam raptores ad culpam. Neque enim Eva lignum vetitum contigisset, nisi hoc prius incaute respiceret. Scriptum quippe est: Vidit mulier quod bonum esset lignum ad vescendum, et pulchrum oculis, aspectuque delectabile; et tulit de fructu ejus, et comedit (Gen. III, 6).
chunks.english: That the pure mind may be preserved in thought, the eyes must be cast down from the wantonness of their pleasure, as if they were certain robbers to guilt. For Eve would not have touched the forbidden tree unless she had first looked at it carelessly. For it is written in Genesis, chapter three, verse six: The woman saw that the tree was good for food and beautiful to the eyes and delightful to behold, and she took of its fruit and ate.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Pensandum summopere est quanto debemus moderamine erga illicita visum restringere nos, qui mortaliter vivimus, si et Eva mater viventium per oculos ad mortem venit.
chunks.english: We must consider most especially with what restraint we who live mortally must restrict our sight toward illicit things, if even Eve, mother of the living, came to death through her eyes.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sub Judaeae voce, quae, exteriora videndo concupiscens, bona interiora perdiderat, propheta dicit: Oculus meus depraedatus est animam meam (Thren. III, 51). Concupiscendo enim visibilia, invisibiles virtutes amisit. Quae ergo interiorem fructum per exteriorem visum perdidit, per oculum corporis pertulit praedam cordis.
chunks.english: Under the voice of Judea, which, desiring by seeing outward things, had lost inner goods, the prophet says in Lamentations, chapter three, verse fifty-one: My eye has plundered my soul. For by desiring visible things, it lost invisible virtues. Therefore, she who lost inner fruit through outer sight bore the plunder of the heart through the eye of the body.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum in Evangelio est: Quod omnis qui viderit mulierem ad concupiscendum eam, jam maechatus est eam in corde suo (Matth. V, 27; Moral. XXI, c. 2, n. 5). Per Mosem quippe luxuria perpetrata, per auctorem vero munditiae luxuria cogitata damnatur.
chunks.english: It is written in the Gospel of Matthew, chapter five, verse twenty-seven: Everyone who looks at a woman to lust after her has already committed adultery with her in his heart. Through Moses, lust performed is condemned, but through the author of purity, lust thought is condemned.
chunks.notes: Text should cite Matthew 5:28, but following source as given.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXIV. De gulae concupiscentia.
chunks.english: Chapter 24: The Desire of Gluttony.
chunks.notes: Continuing to the next major sections.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sciendum est quia quinque nos modis gulae vitium tentat (Moral. lib. XXX, c. 18, n. 60). Aliquando namque indigentiae tempora praevenit; aliquando vero tempus non praevenit, sed cibos lautiores quaerit; aliquando quaelibet sumenda sint, praeparari accuratius expetit; aliquando autem et qualitati ciborum, et tempori, congruit, sed in ipsa quantitate sumendi mensuram refectionis excedit.
chunks.english: We must know that the vice of gluttony tempts us in five ways. Sometimes it anticipates the times of need. Sometimes it does not anticipate the time, but seeks more sumptuous foods. Sometimes, whatever is to be consumed, it desires to be prepared more carefully. Sometimes it agrees with both the quality of foods and the time, but exceeds the measure of moderate refreshment in the very quantity of consumption.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam vero et abjectius est quod desiderat, et tamen ipso esu immensi desiderii deterius peccat.
chunks.english: Sometimes what it desires is even more lowly, yet it sins worse by the very burning of immense desire.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Vitiorum tempora melius ostendimus, si haec exemplis evidentioribus approbemus. Mortis quippe sententiam patris ore Jonathas meruit, quia in gustu mellis constitutum edendi tempus antecessit (I Reg. XIV, 27).
chunks.english: We show the times of vices better if we prove these things by clearer examples. For Jonathan earned the sentence of death from his father's mouth because in tasting honey he went before the established time for eating, as told in the First Book of Samuel, chapter fourteen, verse twenty-seven.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ex Aegypto populus eductus in eremo occubuit, quia, despecto manna, cibos carnium petiit, quos lautiores putavit (Num. XXI, 5).
chunks.english: The people led out of Egypt died in the desert because, despising the manna, they sought foods of meat which they considered more sumptuous, as recounted in Numbers, chapter twenty-one, verse five.
chunks.notes: Context suggests Numbers 11 might be more accurate, but following source text.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Prima filiorum Heli culpa suborta est quod ex eorum voto sacerdotis puer non antiquo more coctas vellet de sacrificio carnes accipere, sed crudas quaereret, quas accuratius exhiberet (I Reg. II, 12 seq.).
chunks.english: The first guilt of the sons of Eli arose because according to their wish the servant of the priest would not receive cooked meats from the sacrifice in the ancient manner, but sought raw ones which he would present more carefully, as told in the First Book of Samuel, chapter two, verse twelve and following.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum ad Jerusalem dicitur: Haec fuit iniquitas Sodomae sororis tuae, superbia, saturitas panis, et abundantia (Ezech. XVI, 49), aperte ostenditur quod idcirco salutem perdidit, quia cum superbiae vitio mensuram moderatae refectionis excessit (Gen. XXV, 33).
chunks.english: When it is said to Jerusalem in Ezekiel, chapter sixteen, verse forty-nine: This was the iniquity of your sister Sodom: pride, fullness of bread, and abundance, it is openly shown that she therefore lost salvation because with the vice of pride she exceeded the measure of moderate refreshment.
chunks.notes: Genesis 25:33 reference seems misplaced; may be scribal error in source.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Primogenitorum gloriam Esau ideo amisit, quia magno aestu desiderii vilem cibum, id est, lenticulam concupivit: quam dum venditis etiam primogenitis praetulit quo in illam appetitu anhelaret indicavit.
chunks.english: Esau therefore lost the glory of the firstborn because with great burning of desire he coveted vile food, that is, lentils, which when he preferred even to the rights of the firstborn being sold, he indicated with what appetite he panted for it.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Neque enim cibus, sed appetitus in vitio est. Unde et lautiores cibos plerumque sine culpa sumimus, et abjectiores non sine reatu conscientiae degustamus.
chunks.english: For it is not the food but the appetite that is in the vice. Therefore we often take more sumptuous foods without guilt, and taste lowlier ones not without blame of conscience.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Esau primatum per esum lenticulae perdidit, et Elias in eremo virtutem corporis carnes edendo servavit (III Reg. XVII, 6).
chunks.english: Esau lost the primacy through eating lentils, and Elijah in the desert preserved the strength of his body by eating meat, as told in the Third Book of Kings, chapter seventeen, verse six.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Antiquus hostis, quia non cibum, sed cibi concupiscentiam esse causam damnationis intelligit, primum sibi hominem non carne, sed pomo subdidit, et secundum non carne, sed pane tentavit (Gen. III, 6; Matth. IV, 3).
chunks.english: The ancient enemy, because he understands that not food but the desire for food is the cause of damnation, subjected the first man to himself not by meat but by fruit, and tempted the second not by meat but by bread, as told in Genesis, chapter three, verse six, and Matthew, chapter four, verse three.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque primi parentis culpa committitur, etiam cum abjecta et vilia sumuntur. Neque enim Adam solus, ut a vetito se pomo suspenderet, praeceptum prohibitionis accepit; nam cum alimenta quaedam saluti nostrae Deus contraria indicat, ab his nos quasi per sententiam revocat.
chunks.english: Often the guilt of the first parent is committed even when lowly and cheap things are consumed. For Adam alone did not receive the command of prohibition to refrain from the forbidden fruit. For when God indicates certain foods as contrary to our health, he calls us back from these as if through a sentence.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Et cum concupiscentes noxia attingimus, profecto quid aliud quam vetita degustamus? Ea in cibo sumenda sunt, quae naturae necessitas quaerit, non quae edendi libido suggerit, ne si haec moderata discretio minus cante prospiciat, illicitae se concupiscentiae quis voragine immergat (Moral. XXX, c. 18, n. 61).
chunks.english: And when we touch harmful things with desire, what else do we taste but forbidden things? Those things are to be taken in food which the necessity of nature requires, not what the lust of eating suggests, lest if moderate discretion looks ahead less carefully, someone may immerse himself in the abyss of illicit desire.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Gulae deditos superfluitas locutionis, levitas operis, atque luxuria comitatur (Reg. Past. part. III, c. 19). Nisi gulae deditos immoderata loquacitas raperet, dives, qui epulatus quotidie splendide dicitur, in lingua gravius non arderet.
chunks.english: Those devoted to gluttony are accompanied by superfluity of speech, levity of work, and lust. Unless immoderate talkativeness seized those devoted to gluttony, the rich man who is said to have feasted splendidly every day would not burn more severely in his tongue.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ait enim: Pater Abraham, miserere mei, et mitte Lazarum, ut intingat extremum digiti sui in aqua, et refrigeret linguam meam, quia crucior in hac flamma (Luc. XVI, 24).
chunks.english: For he says in Luke, chapter sixteen, verse twenty-four: Father Abraham, have mercy on me and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue, for I am tormented in this flame.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quibus profecto verbis ostenditur quia epulando quotidie crebrius in lingua peccaverat, qui totus ardens refrigerari se praecipue in lingua requirebat.
chunks.english: By these words it is certainly shown that by feasting daily he had sinned more frequently in his tongue, he who burning entirely required to be cooled especially in his tongue.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quia gulae deditos levitas protinus operis sequitur, auctoritas sacra testatur, dicens: Sedit populus manducare, et bibere, et surrexerunt ludere (Exod. XXXII, 6).
chunks.english: Because levity of work immediately follows those devoted to gluttony, sacred authority testifies, saying in Exodus, chapter thirty-two, verse six: The people sat down to eat and drink, and rose up to play.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque edacitas usque ad luxuriam pertrahit, quia dum satietate venter extenditur, aculei libidinis excitantur.
chunks.english: Often gluttony drags one even to lust, because when the belly is extended by satiety, the goads of desire are aroused.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hosti callido, qui primi hominis sensum in concupiscentia pomi aperuit, sed in peccati laqueo strinxit, divina voce dicitur: Pectore et ventre repes (Gen. III, 14). Ac si ei aperte diceretur: Cogitatione et ingluvie super humana corda dominaberis.
chunks.english: To the cunning enemy, who opened the sense of the first man in the desire for fruit but bound him in the snare of sin, it is said by divine voice in Genesis, chapter three, verse fourteen: On your breast and belly you shall crawl. As if it were openly said to him: By thought and gluttony you shall dominate over human hearts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quia gulae deditos luxuria sequitur, propheta testatur; qui dum aperta narrat, occulta denuntiat, dicens: Princeps coquorum dextruxit muros Jerusalem (Jerem. XXXIX, sec. text. Hebraeum, et sec. LXX).
chunks.english: Because lust follows those devoted to gluttony, the prophet testifies, who while narrating open things, denounces hidden things, saying according to the Hebrew text and the Seventy in Jeremiah, chapter thirty-nine: The chief of the cooks destroyed the walls of Jerusalem.
chunks.notes: Modern reference would be Jeremiah 52:14 or 2 Kings 25:10.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Princeps namque coquorum venter est, cui magna cura obsequium a coquis impenditur, ut ipse delectabiliter cibis impleatur. Muri autem Jerusalem virtutes sunt animae, ad desiderium supernae pacis elevatae. Coquorum igitur princeps muros Jerusalem dejicit, quia dum venter ingluvie tenditur, virtutes animae per luxuriam destruuntur.
chunks.english: For the chief of the cooks is the belly, to which great care of service is rendered by cooks, so that it itself may be filled delightfully with foods. But the walls of Jerusalem are the virtues of the soul, elevated to the desire of heavenly peace. Therefore the chief of the cooks casts down the walls of Jerusalem, because while the belly is stretched by gluttony, the virtues of the soul are destroyed through lust.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.chapter_title: The Battle of Virtues Against Vices
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXV. De pugna virtutum adversus vitiae.
chunks.english: Chapter 25: The Battle of Virtues Against Vices.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum domino vitiorum contradicimus, cum iniquitati, quae nos a Deo separat, reluctamur, cum consuetudini fortiter et violenter resistimus, et, desideria perversa calcantes, contra hanc jus nobis libertatis ingenitae vindicamus, vitiorum agminibus acerrimo conflictu resistimus (Moral. IV, c. 36, n. 71).
chunks.english: When we contradict the lord of vices, when we struggle against iniquity which separates us from God, when we resist custom strongly and violently, and trampling perverse desires, vindicate for ourselves against it the right of innate liberty, we resist the armies of vices in the fiercest conflict.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum culpas poenitendo percutimus, et maculas sordium fletibus lavamus, fortiter contra vitia reluctamur.
chunks.english: When we strike down faults by repenting and wash away the stains of filth with tears, we struggle strongly against vices.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tunc se viri sancti veracius a vitiorum colluvione detergunt, dum ab eis contra singula vitia virtutes singulae opponuntur (Apud Isid., lib. II, sentent. c. 37).
chunks.english: Then holy men more truly cleanse themselves from the flood of vices when individual virtues are opposed by them against individual vices.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Interdum vitia cum virtutibus ad utilitatem confligunt, ut ipso certamine, vel mens exerceatur, vel ab elatione concussus animus restringatur.
chunks.english: Sometimes vices conflict with virtues for benefit, so that by the very contest, either the mind is exercised, or the soul shaken from pride is restrained.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Adversus impetus vitiorum contrariis virtutibus est pugnandum. Contra luxuriam enim cordis est adhibenda munditia, contra odium dilectio praeparanda, contra iracundiam patientia proponenda est; porro contra timorem fiduciae adhibenda est virtus, contra torporem zeli praelium.
chunks.english: We must fight against the assaults of vices with contrary virtues. For against lust of heart, purity must be applied; against hatred, love must be prepared; against anger, patience must be set forth. Furthermore, against fear, the virtue of confidence must be applied; against torpor, the battle of zeal.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tristitiae quoque gaudium, accidiae fortitudo, avaritiae largitas, superbiae humilitas opponenda est. Sicque singulae virtutes nascentia contra se vitia reprimunt, ac tentationum motus virtute divinae charitatis exstinguunt.
chunks.english: Also joy must be opposed to sadness, fortitude to sloth, generosity to avarice, humility to pride. And thus individual virtues repress the vices arising against them, and extinguish the movements of temptations by the virtue of divine charity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Libidinem abstinentia domat. Nam quantum corpus inedia frangitur, tantum mens ab illicito appetitu revocatur. Adversus iram tolerantia dimicat. Ira autem semetipsam necat, sustinendo autem patientia victoriam portat.
chunks.english: Abstinence tames lust. For to the degree that the body is broken by fasting, to that degree the mind is called back from illicit appetite. Tolerance fights against anger. But anger kills itself, and by enduring, patience carries victory.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Tristitiae moerorem spes aeterni gaudii superat; et quem turbata mens de exterioribus afficit, dulcedo interioris tranquillitatis lenit. Adversus invidiam praeparetur charitas, et adversus irae incendia mansuetudinis adhibeatur tranquillitas.
chunks.english: Hope of eternal joy overcomes the grief of sadness; and whom the troubled mind afflicts concerning exterior things, the sweetness of inner tranquility soothes. Against envy let charity be prepared, and against the fires of anger let the tranquility of gentleness be applied.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXX. De luxuria.
chunks.english: Chapter 30: The Sin of Lust

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Malum luxuriae aut cogitatione perpetratur, aut opere. Callidus namque adversarius noster, cum ab effectu operis expellitur, secreta poliuere cogitatione molitur. Serpenti a Domino dicitur: Pectore et ventre repes. Serpens videlicet ventre repit, quando hostis lubricus per humana membra sibimet subdita usque ad expletionem operis luxuriam exercet.
chunks.english: The evil of lust is committed either by thought or by deed. When our cunning adversary is expelled from accomplishing the physical act, he secretly works to pollute our thoughts. The Lord says to the serpent: You shall crawl on your breast and belly. The serpent crawls on its belly when the slippery enemy exercises lust through human members subjected to him all the way to the completion of the deed.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Serpens autem repit pectore, quando eos quos in opere luxuriae non valet, in cogitatione polluit. Quisquis luxuriam perpetrat actione, huic serpens repit ex ventre; quisquis autem perpetrandam versat in mente, huic serpens repit ex pectore. Ardentem quasi Sodomam fugere, est illicita carnis incendia declinare.
chunks.english: But the serpent crawls on its breast when it pollutes in thought those whom it cannot corrupt in lustful action. Whoever commits lust in deed—for him the serpent crawls from the belly. Whoever turns it over in his mind—for him the serpent crawls from the breast. To flee as from burning Sodom is to turn away from the unlawful fires of the flesh.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quisquis mundi hujus successibus elevatus lenocinante cordis laetitia tentari se luxuriae stimulis sentit, Joseph factum ad memoriam revocet, et in arce se castitatis servet. Cum voluptas lubrica tentat in prosperis, haec ipsa sunt prospera aculeo tentationis opponenda, ut eos erubescamus prava committere, quo nos a Deo meminimus gratuita bona percipere.
chunks.english: Whoever is lifted up by worldly success and feels himself tempted by the goads of lust through the seduction of heartfelt pleasure should call to mind what Joseph did, and guard himself in the fortress of chastity. When slippery pleasure tempts us in prosperity, we must set these very prosperities against the sting of temptation, so that we are ashamed to commit evil in the very moment we remember receiving gratuitous goods from God.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnunquam voluptas, quae ex prosperitate nascitur, ejusdem prosperitatis est consideratione ferienda, quatenus hostis noster, unde oritur, inde moriatur. Propheta David subito casu per luxuriam defluxit, dum in solario deambulans alienam conjugem concupivit et abstulit; ejusque virum cum damno sui exercitus interemit, et repentino casu cecidit, cum mens illa mysteriis coelestibus assueta ab inopinata tentatione devicta est, atque immanissimae turpitudini subacta.
chunks.english: Sometimes the pleasure born from prosperity must be struck down by considering that very prosperity, so that our enemy dies from the same source that gave him birth. The prophet David fell suddenly through lust when, walking on his rooftop, he desired and seized another man's wife, then killed her husband at the cost of his own army. He fell in sudden collapse when that mind, accustomed to heavenly mysteries, was conquered by unexpected temptation and subjugated to monstrous depravity. As the Second Book of Samuel, chapter eleven, verse four recounts.
chunks.notes: Citation: 2 Samuel (Vulgate: II Regum) 11:4, followed by Gregory's Moralia XII.18.23

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quasi saxum de loco suo translatum est, cum prophetae animus a prophetiae mysteriis exclusus ad cogitandas turpitudines venit. Salomon ille quondam sapientissimus nimietate luxuriae superatus immoderato usu atque assiduitate mulierum ad hoc usque perductus est, ut templum idolis fabricaret; et qui prius Deo templum construxerat, assiduitate libidinis etiam perfidiae substratus idolis construere templa non timeret.
chunks.english: He was like a stone moved from its place when the prophet's soul, shut out from the mysteries of prophecy, turned to thinking about depravities. Solomon, once the wisest of men, was overcome by excessive lust and led by immoderate and constant use of women to the point where he built a temple for idols. He who had previously constructed a temple for God was not afraid, through the constancy of his lust and subjection to faithlessness, to construct temples for idols.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicque factum est ut ab assidua carnis petulantia usque ad mentis perfidiam perveniret, quia, subripiente paulisper infusione peccati, terra cordis illius ad consummationem defluxit. Non immerito jumenta terrae nuncupantur qui hujus vitae carnalis ima appetunt, quos in infimis illecebrosa voluptas dejicit, quibus per sacra eloquia dicitur: Nolite fieri sicut equus et mulus, in quibus non est intellectus.
chunks.english: And so it happened that from constant carnal wantonness he arrived at faithlessness of mind, because as sin's influence gradually crept in, the soil of his heart flowed away to complete ruin. Not undeservedly are those called beasts of the earth who seek the lowest things of this carnal life, whom enticing pleasure casts down into the depths, to whom it is said in sacred Scripture: Do not become like the horse and mule, in which there is no understanding. As the Psalm, thirty-one, verse nine says.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Voluptatum carnis amatoribus dicitur: Mortificate nunc membra vestra, quae sunt super terram; id est, fornicationem, immunditiam, libidinem, concupiscentiam malam, et avaritiam. Paulus apostolus petulantiae carnis deditos publica invectione redarguit dicens: Cum cognovissent Deum, non sicut Deum glorificaverunt, aut gratias egerunt, sed evanuerunt in cogitationibus suis.
chunks.english: To lovers of fleshly pleasures it is said: Put to death now your members which are upon the earth, that is, fornication, uncleanness, lust, evil desire, and avarice. As Paul writes in his letter to the Colossians, chapter three, verse five. Paul the apostle rebukes those devoted to carnal wantonness with public denunciation, saying: When they had known God, they did not glorify him as God or give thanks, but became vain in their thoughts. As he writes in his letter to the Romans, chapter one, verse twenty-one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quomodo autem in jumentorum aut plusquam jumentorum voluptate ceciderunt, subdidit dicens: Tradidit illos in desideria cordis eorum in immunditiam. Nonnullos per luxuriam caro in infimis mersit, quos superba in altum scientia sublevavit, et a volatu volucrum ultra appetitum lapsi sunt jumentorum.
chunks.english: How they fell into the pleasure of beasts, or even worse than beasts, he added by saying: He delivered them up to the desires of their heart unto uncleanness, as Romans, chapter one, verse twenty-four says. Some were plunged by the flesh into the depths through lust, whom proud knowledge had lifted up on high, and they fell from the flight of birds beyond desire into the appetite of beasts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Atque inde sub se prostrati sunt, unde super se ire videbantur. Plerumque virus libidinis de radice nascitur elationis; tunc ergo caro vincit, cum spiritus latenter intumescit, quia tunc anima per originem culpae in jumentorum petulantiam cecidit, cum efferendo se more volucrum ultra quam debuit evolavit.
chunks.english: And they were cast down below themselves from the very place where they seemed to be going above themselves. Often the poison of lust is born from the root of pride. The flesh conquers when the spirit secretly swells, because the soul fell into the wantonness of beasts through the origin of fault when, exalting itself in the manner of birds, it flew higher than it should have.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Interdum per elationis vitium longa continentia repente dissolvitur, et plerumque usque ad senium virginitas servata vitiatur. Quia etenim negligitur humilitas cordis, rectus judex despicit etiam integritatem corporis; et quandoque per apertum malum reprobos annuntiat, quos dudum reprobos in occulto tolerabat.
chunks.english: Sometimes through the vice of pride, long continence is suddenly dissolved, and often virginity preserved until old age is corrupted. Because humility of heart is neglected, the righteous judge scorns even bodily integrity. And sometimes through open evil he announces as reprobate those whom he had long tolerated as reprobate in secret.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quisquis diu servatum bonum subito perdidit, apud semetipsum intus aliud malum tenuit, ex quo ad aliud subito erupit, per quod ab omnipotente Deo etiam tunc alienus exstitit, quando se ei per munditiam corporis inhaerere monstravit. Plerumque mentis elatio ad pollutionem pertrahit carnis, et reproborum cor a volatu volucrum ad petulantiam mergit jumentorum.
chunks.english: Whoever suddenly lost a good long preserved held within himself another evil, from which he suddenly burst forth into another, through which he stood alien from almighty God even when he showed himself clinging to God through bodily purity. Often mental pride drags one down to pollution of the flesh, and plunges the heart of the reprobate from the flight of birds to the wantonness of beasts.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sciendum nobis magnopere est, aliud esse, quod animus de tentatione carnis patitur, aliud, cum per consensum delectationibus obligatur. Plerumque enim cogitatione prava pulsatur, sed renititur. Plerumque autem cum perversum quid concipit, hoc intra semetipsum etiam per desiderium volvit. Et nimirum mentem nequaquam cogitatio immunda inquinat cum pulsat, sed cum hanc sibi per delectationem subjugat.
chunks.english: We must know well that it is one thing what the mind suffers from temptation of the flesh, and another when it is bound to pleasures through consent. Often it is struck by perverse thought but resists. Often when it conceives something perverse, it turns this over within itself even through desire. And certainly an unclean thought in no way pollutes the mind when it knocks, but when it subjugates the mind to itself through delight.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Paulus praedicator egregius ait: Tentatio vos non apprehendat nisi humana. Humana quippe tentatio est qua plerumque in cogitatione tangimur etiam nolentes, quia ut nonnunquam et illicita ad animum veniant, hoc utique in nobismetipsis ex humanitatis corruptibilis pondere habemus. Daemoniaca est et non humana tentatio, cum ad hoc quod carnis corruptibilitas suggerit, per consensum se animus astringit.
chunks.english: Paul the excellent preacher says: Let no temptation take hold of you but such as is human. As he writes in his first letter to the Corinthians, chapter ten, verse thirteen. Human temptation is that by which we are often touched in thought even unwillingly, because when sometimes even unlawful things come to the mind, we certainly have this in ourselves from the weight of corruptible humanity. Demonic and not human is the temptation when the mind binds itself by consent to what the corruptibility of flesh suggests.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem Paulus apostolus ait: Non regnet peccatum in vestro mortali corpore. Peccatum quippe in mortali corpore non esse, sed regnare prohibuit, quia in carne corruptibili non regnare potest, sed non esse non potest. Humano generi de peccato tentari peccatum est. Quo quia quandiu vivimus perfecte omnimodo non caremus, sancta praedicatio, quoniam hoc expellere non potuit, ei de nostro cordis habitaculo regnum tulit, ut appetitus illicitus etsi plerumque bonis nostris cogitationibus occulte se quasi fur inserit, saltem, si ingreditur, non dominetur.
chunks.english: The same apostle Paul says: Let not sin reign in your mortal body, as in his letter to the Romans, chapter six, verse twelve. He forbade sin to reign in the mortal body, not to exist there, because in corruptible flesh it cannot reign but cannot help but exist. For the human race, to be tempted by sin is sin. Because as long as we live we cannot be perfectly free of this in every way, holy preaching, since it could not expel this, took away its kingdom from the dwelling place of our heart, so that even if illicit appetite often inserts itself secretly like a thief into our good thoughts, at least if it enters, it does not dominate.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In Genesi scriptum est: Dominus pluit super Sodomam et Gomorrham sulphur et ignem. Quid in sulphure nisi fetor carnis, et quid per ignem nisi ardor desiderii carnalis exprimitur? Cum ergo habitantium Sodomis vel Gomorrha carnis scelera punire Dominus decrevisset, in ipsa qualitate ultionis notavit maculam criminis. Sulphur quippe fetorem habet, ignis ardorem. Qui itaque ad perversa desideria ex carnis fetore arserant, dignum fuit ut simul sulphure et igne perirent, quatenus ex justa poena discerent ex injusto desiderio quid fecissent.
chunks.english: In Genesis it is written: The Lord rained down sulfur and fire upon Sodom and Gomorrah, as in Genesis, chapter nineteen, verse twenty-four. What is expressed in sulfur but the stench of flesh, and what by fire but the burning of carnal desire? When the Lord decreed to punish the fleshly crimes of those dwelling in Sodom or Gomorrah, in the very quality of the punishment he marked the stain of the crime. Sulfur has a stench, fire has burning. Those who had burned with perverse desires from the stench of flesh, it was fitting that they perish by both sulfur and fire together, so that from just punishment they might learn what they had done from unjust desire.
chunks.notes: Citation: Genesis 19:24, with Gregory's Moralia XIV.19.23

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXI. De torpore animi.
chunks.english: Chapter 31: Spiritual Torpor and Negligence

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque mens hominis a cura suae sollicitudinis dormiens verberatur et non dolet, quia sicut imminentia mala non prospicit, sic neque quae perpetraverit agnoscit. Trahitur et nequaquam sentit, quia per illecebras vitiorum ducitur, nec tamen ad sui custodiam suscitatur.
chunks.english: Often the human mind, sleeping from concern for its own watchfulness, is struck and feels no pain, because just as it does not foresee coming evils, so neither does it recognize what it has done. It is dragged along and feels nothing, because it is led by the enticements of vices yet is not roused to guard itself.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quamvis somno torporis a sui custodia quisque prematur, vigilare tamen ad saeculi curas nititur, ut semper voluptatibus debrietur. Et cum ad illud dormiat, in quo solerter vigilare debuerat, ad aliud vigilare appetit, ad quod laudabiliter dormire potuisset.
chunks.english: Although one is weighed down by the sleep of torpor from guarding oneself, one strives to stay awake for worldly concerns, so as always to be intoxicated by pleasures. And when one sleeps concerning that in which one ought skillfully to keep watch, one desires to be awake for something else, for which one could laudably have slept.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Salomonem in Proverbiis dicitur: Et eris quasi dormiens in medio mari, et quasi sopitus gubernator amisso clavo. In medio enim mari dormit, qui in hujus mundi tentationibus positus praevidere motus irruentium vitiorum quasi imminentes undarum cumulos negligit. Et quasi gubernator clavum amittit, quando mens ad regendam navem corporis studium sollicitudinis perdit.
chunks.english: Through Solomon in Proverbs it is said: And you will be like one sleeping in the middle of the sea, and like a drowsy helmsman who has lost the rudder. As Proverbs, chapter twenty-three, verse thirty-four records. For he sleeps in the middle of the sea who, placed in the temptations of this world, neglects to foresee the movements of rushing vices like impending heaps of waves. And he loses the rudder like a helmsman when the mind loses its diligent care for steering the ship of the body.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Clavum in mari amittere est intentionem providam inter procellas hujus saeculi non tenere. Si enim gubernator clavum sollicite stringit, modo in fluctibus ex adverso navem dirigit, modo ventorum impetus per obliquum findit.
chunks.english: To lose the rudder at sea is not to maintain provident intention amid the storms of this age. For if the helmsman holds the rudder carefully, now he steers the ship directly against the waves, now he cuts through the force of the winds at an angle.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scriptum est: Stellio manibus nititur, et moratur in aedibus regis. Plerumque aves, quas ad volatum penna sublevat, in vepribus resident; et stellio, qui ad volatum pennas non habet, nitens manibus regni aedificium tenet. Quia nimirum saepe ingeniosi quique, dum per negligentiam torpent, in pravis actibus remanent; et simplices, quos ingenii penna non adjuvat, ad obtinenda regni aeterni moenia virtus operationis levat.
chunks.english: It is written: The lizard strives with its hands, and dwells in the palaces of the king. As Proverbs, chapter thirty, verse twenty-eight says. Often birds, which the feather lifts to flight, dwell in thickets; and the lizard, which has no feathers for flight, striving with its hands holds the building of the kingdom. Because certainly often clever people, while they grow torpid through negligence, remain in evil deeds; and simple people, whom the feather of talent does not help, the virtue of action lifts to obtain the walls of the eternal kingdom.
chunks.notes: Citation: Proverbs 30:28 (Vulgate gives verse 23, but this appears to be 30:28 in modern numbering)

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Stellio, dum manibus nititur, in regis aedibus moratur, quia illo simplex per intentionem recti operis pervenit quo ingeniosus minime ascendit. Quaestio suboritur cur vel negligenti intelligentiae donum tribuitur, vel studiosus quisque sensus sui tarditate praepeditur.
chunks.english: The lizard, while striving with its hands, dwells in the king's buildings, because the simple person arrives there through the intention of right action where the clever person scarcely ascends. A question arises: why is the gift of understanding granted to the negligent, or why is any diligent person hindered by slowness of perception?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ad quam citius respondetur, dum protinus per Salomonem subditur: Nihil in terra sine causa. Idcirco enim saepe et desidiosus ingenium accipit, unde negligens etiam justius puniatur. Quia quod sine labore assequi potuit, scire contemnit. Et idcirco nonnunquam studiosus tarditate intelligentiae premitur, ut eo majora praemia retributionis inveniat, quo magis in studio inventionis elaborat.
chunks.english: To which the answer is quickly given, when it is immediately added: Nothing on earth is without cause. As the book of Job, chapter five, verse six says. For often the lazy person receives talent, from which the negligent person is even more justly punished. Because what he could attain without labor, he scorns to know. And therefore sometimes the diligent person is burdened by slowness of understanding, so that he may find greater rewards of recompense the more he labors in the study of discovery.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nihil ergo est in terra sine causa, quando et studioso tarditas ad praemium proficit, et desiderio velocitas ad supplicium crescit. Mentis desidia, dum congruo fervore non accenditur, a bonorum desiderio funditus, convalescente furtim torpore, mactatur.
chunks.english: Therefore nothing is on earth without cause, when both slowness profits the diligent person toward reward, and swiftness grows toward punishment for the lazy person. Mental sloth, when not kindled by appropriate fervor, is utterly slain from the desire of good things as torpor secretly grows strong.
