chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Salomonem dicitur: Pigredo immittit soporem. Piger enim recte sentiendo quasi vigilat, quamvis nihil operando torpescat; sed pigredo soporem immittere dicitur, quia paulisper etiam recte sentiendi vigilantia amittitur, dum a bene operandi studio cessatur.
chunks.english: Through Solomon it is said: Laziness sends deep sleep. As Proverbs, chapter nineteen, verse fifteen records. For the lazy person seemingly keeps watch by right perception, though he grows torpid by doing nothing; but laziness is said to send deep sleep because gradually even the vigilance of right perception is lost when one ceases from the zeal of doing good.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: De torpore animi recte in Proverbiis scriptum est: Anima dissoluta esuriet. Quisquis se ad superiora distringendo non dirigit, neglectum se inferius per desideria expandit; et dum studiorum sublimium vigore non constringitur, cupiditatis infimae fame sauciatur, ut quo se per disciplinam ligare dissimulat, eo se esuriens per voluptatum desideria spargat.
chunks.english: Concerning torpor of the soul it is rightly written in Proverbs: The slack soul shall hunger. Whoever does not direct himself upward by constraint expands himself downward through desires by neglect; and while not constrained by the vigor of lofty pursuits, he is wounded by the hunger of base desire, so that where he refuses to bind himself through discipline, there he scatters himself hungering through desires for pleasures.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hinc ab eodem rursus Salomone scribitur: In desideriis est omnis otiosus. Hinc ipsa Veritate praedicante, uno quidem exeunte spiritu, munda domus dicitur, sed multiplicius redeunte, dum vacat, occupatur.
chunks.english: Hence by the same Solomon it is written again: Every idle person is in desires. As Proverbs, chapter twenty-one, verse twenty-six says. Hence with Truth itself preaching, when one spirit goes out, the house is said to be clean, but when it returns multiplied, while it is vacant, it is occupied. As the Gospel of Matthew, chapter twelve, verses forty-four and forty-five records.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXII. De pigritia.
chunks.english: Chapter 32: The Vice of Laziness

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Suadendi sunt pigri, ne agenda bona, dum differunt, amittant; et dum opportune agere quae possunt nolunt, paulo post, cum volunt, non valeant. Plerumque piger, dum necessaria agere negligit, quaedam sibi difficilia opponit, quaedam vero incaute formidat; et dum quasi invenit quod velut juste metuat, ostendit quod in otio quasi non injuste torpescat.
chunks.english: The lazy must be persuaded lest they lose good things to be done by delaying them, and lest when they are unwilling to do opportunely what they can, shortly after, when they wish to, they are unable. Often the lazy person, while neglecting to do necessary things, sets up certain difficulties for himself, and carelessly fears certain things; and while he seemingly finds what he might justly fear, he shows that he does not unjustly grow torpid in idleness.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Pigro etenim per Salomonem dicitur: Propter frigus piger arare noluit; mendicabit ergo aestate, et non dabitur ei. Propter frigus quippe piger non arat, dum desidiae torpore constrictus, agere quae debet bona dissimulat. Et dum parva ex adverso mala metuit, operari maxima praetermittit. Bene autem subditur: Mendicabit aestate, et non dabitur ei.
chunks.english: For to the lazy person it is said through Solomon: Because of the cold the lazy person refused to plow; therefore he will beg in summer, and it will not be given to him. As Proverbs, chapter twenty, verse four says. Because of the cold indeed the lazy person does not plow, while constrained by the torpor of sloth, he pretends not to do the good things he ought. And while he fears small evils from opposition, he neglects to work the greatest things. But well is it added: He will beg in summer, and it will not be given to him.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui enim nunc in bonis operibus non exsudat, cum sol judicii ferventior apparuerit, quia frustra regni aditum postulat, nil accipiens aestate mendicat. Hinc iterum scriptum est: Abscondit piger manum suam sub ascella, nec ad os suum porrigit eam. Nemo itaque tam piger est, ut ad os suum manum vel pro comedendo reducere laborem putet.
chunks.english: For he who does not now sweat in good works, when the more fervent sun of judgment appears, because he seeks in vain the entrance to the kingdom, begs in summer receiving nothing. Hence it is written again: The lazy person hides his hand under his armpit, and does not bring it to his mouth. As Proverbs, chapter nineteen, verse twenty-four says. Therefore no one is so lazy that he considers it labor to bring his hand to his mouth even for eating.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed piger nec ad os suum manum suam porrigit, qui nec hoc vult operari quod dicit. Per manum quippe operatio, per os autem locutio designatur. Pigro labor est ad os manum suam porrigere, quia desidiosus quisque praedicator nec hoc vult operari quod dicit. Manum quippe ad os porrigere, est voci suae opere concordare.
chunks.english: But the lazy person does not bring his hand to his mouth, because he does not even want to do what he says. For by the hand action is designated, but by the mouth speech. It is labor for the lazy person to bring his hand to his mouth, because any slothful preacher does not even want to do what he says. To bring the hand to the mouth is to make one's action accord with one's voice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Idem Salomon ait: Per agrum hominis pigri transivi, et per vineam viri stulti; et ecce totum repleverant urticae: operuerant superficiem ejus spinae, et maceria lapidum destructa erat. Per agrum hominis pigri atque per vineam viri stulti transire, est cujuslibet vitam negligentis inspicere, ejusque opera considerare.
chunks.english: The same Solomon says: I passed by the field of the lazy man, and by the vineyard of the foolish man; and behold, nettles had filled everything: thorns had covered its surface, and the stone wall was destroyed. As Proverbs, chapter twenty-four, verse thirty records. To pass through the field of the lazy man and through the vineyard of the foolish man is to inspect the life of any negligent person and to consider his works.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quam urticae vel spinae replent, quia in corde negligentium prurientia terrena desideria et punctiones pullulant vitiorum. Maceria lapidum destructa erat, id est, disciplina patrum ab ejus corde dissoluta; nam quia destructam maceriam lapidum dissolutam esse disciplinam vidit, illic protinus secutus adjunxit: Quod cum vidissem, posui in corde meo, et exemplo didici disciplinam.
chunks.english: Which nettles or thorns fill, because in the heart of the negligent, itching earthly desires and the stings of vices sprout. The stone wall was destroyed, that is, the discipline of the fathers was dissolved from his heart. For because he saw that the destroyed stone wall was dissolved discipline, immediately following he added: When I had seen this, I laid it up in my heart, and by example I learned discipline. As Proverbs, chapter twenty-four, verse thirty-two says.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum disciplinae vigor in corde reproborum, pigritia dominante, dissolvitur, ante eorum oculos cuncta bonorum opera despectui habentur. Nihilque esse aestimant quidquid de virtutibus electorum vident.
chunks.english: When the vigor of discipline in the heart of the reprobate is dissolved with laziness dominating, all good works are held in contempt before their eyes. And they consider as nothing whatever they see of the virtues of the elect.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXIII. De murmurio.
chunks.english: Chapter 33: The Sin of Murmuring

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Acetum si mittatur in nitro, fervescit nitrum, sicut scriptum est: Sicut acetum in nitro, ita qui cantat cantica cordi pessimo. Perversa mens, quando per increpationem corripitur, aut per praedicationis dulcedinem ad bona suadetur, de correptione fit deterior. Et inde in murmurationis iniquitate succenditur, unde debuit ab iniquitate compesci.
chunks.english: If vinegar is poured on soda, the soda boils up, as it is written: Like vinegar on soda, so is one who sings songs to a wicked heart. The perverse mind, when it is corrected by rebuke or persuaded to good things by the sweetness of preaching, becomes worse from the correction. And it is inflamed in the iniquity of murmuring from that very thing by which it ought to have been restrained from iniquity.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quisquis pro peccato percutitur, nisi murmurando renitatur, eo ipso jam justus esse inchoat, quo ferientis justitiam non accusat.
chunks.english: Whoever is struck for sin, unless he resists by murmuring, begins already to be just by the very fact that he does not accuse the justice of the one striking him.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXIV. De mendacio.
chunks.english: Chapter 34: The Evil of Lying

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plana est omnino veritatis via, et grave est iter mendacii, sicut scriptum est: Docuerunt linguam suam loqui mendacium, ut inique agerent laboraverunt. Quisquis, relicta veritate, mentiri deliberat, ut audientium animum fallat, quantus ei labor est sollicite custodire ne ipsa ejus fallacia deprehendi queat?
chunks.english: The way of truth is entirely level, and the path of lying is burdensome, as it is written: They have taught their tongue to speak lies; they have labored to act wickedly. As the prophet Jeremiah, chapter nine, verse five says. Whoever, abandoning truth, decides to lie in order to deceive the mind of listeners—how great is the labor for him to carefully guard lest his very deception be detected?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ponit quippe ante oculos quid sibi a veritatem scientibus responderi possit, et cum magno cogitatu pertractat quomodo per argumenta falsitatis documenta veritatis exsuperet. Fallacia laborans mendacii hinc inde se circumtegit, et contra hoc, ubi deprehendi potuerit, veritatis similem responsionem quaerit; qui si vellet verum dicere, utique sine labore potuisset.
chunks.english: For he sets before his eyes what might be answered to him by those who know the truth, and with great thought he considers how through arguments of falsehood to overcome the proofs of truth. The laboring deception of lying covers itself on all sides, and against this, where it might be detected, seeks a response similar to truth; who if he wanted to speak truth, certainly could have done so without labor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omne mendacium iniquitas est, et omnis iniquitas mendacium, quia profecto ab aequitate discrepat quidquid a veritate discordat. Nonnunquam pejus est mendacium meditari quam loqui. Nam loqui plerumque praecipitationis est, meditari vero studiosae pravitatis; et quis ignoret in quanta distantia culpa distinguitur: utrum praecipitatione aliquis an studio mentiatur?
chunks.english: Every lie is iniquity, and all iniquity is a lie, because certainly whatever disagrees with truth deviates from equity. Sometimes it is worse to meditate on a lie than to speak it. For to speak is often a matter of rashness, but to meditate is deliberate wickedness; and who does not know in how great a distance the fault is distinguished: whether someone lies from haste or from deliberation?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Summopere cavendum est omne mendacium, quamvis nonnunquam sit aliquod mendacii genus culpae levioris, si quisquam praestando mentiatur. Scriptum est: Os, quod mentitur occidit animam. Et: Perdes eos qui loquuntur mendacium.
chunks.english: Every lie must be avoided with utmost care, although sometimes there is a kind of lie that is a lighter fault, if someone lies to benefit another. It is written: The mouth that lies kills the soul. As the book of Wisdom, chapter one, verse eleven says. And: You will destroy those who speak lies. As the Psalm, five, verse seven records.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc quoque mendacii genus perfecti viri summopere fugiunt, ut nec vita cujuslibet per eorum fallaciam defendatur, nec suae animae noceant, dum praestare carni nituntur alienae, quanquam hoc ipsum peccati genus facillime credimus relaxari. Nam si quaelibet culpa sequenti solet pia operatione purgari, quanto magis haec facile abstergitur, quam mater boni operis pietas ipsa comitatur?
chunks.english: Perfect men also flee this kind of lie with utmost care, so that neither is anyone's life defended through their deception, nor do they harm their own souls while striving to benefit another's flesh, although we believe this very kind of sin is most easily forgiven. For if any fault is usually purged by subsequent pious action, how much more easily is this wiped away, which piety itself, the mother of good work, accompanies?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Nonnulli ex obstetricum fallacia conantur asserere hoc mendacii genus non esse peccatum, maxime quod illis mentientibus scriptum est: Quia aedificavit eis Dominus domos. In obstetricum Aegyptiarum recompensatione cognoscitur quid mendacii culpa mereatur; nam benignitatis earum merces, quae potuit eis in aeterna vita retribui, pro admista culpa mendacii in terrenam est compensationem declinata, ut in vita sua, quam mentiendo tueri voluerunt, ea quae fecerunt bona reciperent, et ulterius quod exspectarent mercedis suae praemium non haberent.
chunks.english: Some try to assert from the deception of the midwives that this kind of lie is not a sin, especially because of them who lied it is written: Because the Lord built houses for them. As the book of Exodus, chapter one, verse twenty-one says. In the recompense of the Egyptian midwives it is known what the fault of lying deserves. For the reward of their kindness, which could have been repaid to them in eternal life, was turned aside into earthly compensation because of the mixed fault of lying, so that in their life, which they wanted to protect by lying, they received the good things they had done, and had no further reward of their recompense to expect.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si subtiliter perpendatur, obstetrices Aegyptiae amore vitae praesentis mentitae sunt, non intentione mercedis. Parcendo quippe conatae sunt infantum vitam tegere, mentiendo suam. Et licet in Testamento Veteri nonnulla possint talia reperiri, pene nunquam tamen hoc vel tale genus mendacii a perfectis admissum studiosus ibi lector inveniet.
chunks.english: If carefully considered, the Egyptian midwives lied from love of the present life, not from intention of reward. For by sparing they attempted to cover the life of the infants by lying about their own actions. And although some such things can be found in the Old Testament, the diligent reader will almost never find this or such a kind of lie admitted by the perfect ones there.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sicut enim aedificium lapidibus, ita mendacium sermonibus fabricatur. Ubi enim non dolosa locutio, sed sensus veritatis est, quasi munita moles non ex fabrica, sed ex natura consurgit. Beatus Job amicis suis non recte sentientibus dicit: Nunquid Deus indiget vestro mendacio, ut pro illo loquamini dolos? Deus mendacio non eget, quia veritas fulciri non quaerit auxilio falsitatis.
chunks.english: For just as a building is constructed with stones, so a lie is fabricated with words. Where there is not deceitful speech but the sense of truth, a fortified mass rises not from construction but from nature. Blessed Job says to his friends who were not thinking rightly: Does God need your lie, that you should speak deceits for him? As the book of Job, chapter thirteen, verse seven records. God does not need a lie, because truth does not seek to be supported by the help of falsehood.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXV. Quibus modis peccatum perpetretur.
chunks.english: Chapter 35: The Four Stages of Sin

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quatuor modis peccatum perpetratur in corde, quatuor consummatur in opere. In corde namque suggestione, delectatione, consensu, et defensionis audacia, perpetratur. Fit enim suggestio per adversarium, delectatio per carnem, consensus per spiritum, defensionis audacia per elationem.
chunks.english: Sin is committed in the heart in four ways, and consummated in deed in four ways. In the heart it is committed by suggestion, delight, consent, and the audacity of defense. Suggestion happens through the adversary, delight through the flesh, consent through the spirit, the audacity of defense through pride.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque culpa, quae terrere mentem debuit, extollit, et dejiciendo elevat, sed gravius elevando supplantat. Unde et illam primi hominis rectitudinem antiquus hostis his quatuor ictibus fregit, nam serpens suasit, Eva delectata est, Adam consensit; qui etiam requisitus confiteri culpam per audaciam noluit.
chunks.english: Often the fault that should have terrified the mind exalts it, and by casting down lifts up, but more gravely by lifting up overthrows. Hence the ancient enemy broke the uprightness of the first man with these four blows: for the serpent persuaded, Eve was delighted, Adam consented; and when questioned he refused through audacity to confess the fault.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc in humano genere quotidie agitur, quod actum in primo parente nostri generis non ignoratur. Serpens suasit, quia occultus hostis mala cordibus hominum latenter suggerit. Eva delectata est, quia carnalis sensus ad verba serpentis mox se delectationi substernit. Assensum vero Adam mulieri ipse praepositus praebuit, quia dum caro in delectationem rapitur, etiam a sua rectitudine spiritus infirmatus inclinatur.
chunks.english: What happened in the first parent of our race is enacted daily in the human race. The serpent persuaded, because the hidden enemy secretly suggests evils to the hearts of people. Eve was delighted, because the carnal sense immediately submits itself to delight at the serpent's words. But Adam himself, placed in charge, gave assent to the woman, because when the flesh is seized by delight, even the spirit, weakened from its uprightness, inclines.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Requisitus Adam confiteri culpam noluit, quia clearly the spirit, which is separated from truth by sinning, is more wickedly hardened in the audacity of its ruin. Per timorem quidem Adam semetipsum absconsurus fugerat; sed tamen requisitus innotuit quantum etiam timens tumebat.
chunks.english: When questioned, Adam refused to confess the fault, because clearly the spirit, which is separated from truth by sinning, is more wickedly hardened in the audacity of its ruin. Indeed Adam had fled to hide himself through fear, but when questioned it became clear how much he swelled even while fearing.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum enim ex peccato praesens poena metuitur, et amissa Dei facies non amatur, timor ex tumore est, non ex humilitate: superbit quippe, qui peccatum, si liceat non puniri, non deserit. Sicut quatuor modis peccatum perpetratur in corde, videlicet suggestione, delectatione, consensu, et defensionis audacia, ita etiam quatuor modis peccatum consummatur in opere.
chunks.english: For when present punishment is feared from sin, and the lost face of God is not loved, the fear comes from swelling, not from humility: for he is proud who does not abandon sin if he could avoid being punished. Just as sin is committed in the heart in four ways—namely suggestion, delight, consent, and the audacity of defense—so also sin is consummated in deed in four ways.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Prius namque latens culpa agitur; postmodum vero etiam ante oculos hominum sine confusione reatus aperitur, dehinc et in consuetudinem ducitur; ad extremum quoque, vel falsae spei seductionibus, vel obstinatione miserae desperationis enutritur.
chunks.english: First the hidden fault is done; afterward the guilt is revealed even before the eyes of people without shame, then it is led into habit; finally it is nourished either by the seductions of false hope or by the obstinacy of wretched desperation.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In primo parente didicimus quia tribus modis omnis culpae nequitiam perpetramus, suggestione scilicet, delectatione, consensu. Primum itaque per hostem, secundum vero per carnem, tertium per spiritum perpetratur. Insidiator enim prava suggerit, caro se delectationi subjicit, atque ad extremum spiritus victus delectatione consentit.
chunks.english: In the first parent we learned that we commit all wickedness of fault in three ways: namely by suggestion, delight, and consent. The first is committed through the enemy, the second through the flesh, the third through the spirit. For the tempter suggests evil things, the flesh subjects itself to delight, and finally the spirit, conquered by delight, consents.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Antiquus serpens in paradiso prava suggessit, Eva autem quasi caro se delectationi subdidit, Adam vero velut spiritus suggestione et delectatione superatus assensit. Suggestione diaboli nonnunquam peccatum agnoscimus, delectatione vincimur, consensu etiam ligamur.
chunks.english: The ancient serpent suggested evil things in paradise, Eve as flesh submitted herself to delight, but Adam as spirit, overcome by suggestion and delight, gave assent. By the devil's suggestion we sometimes recognize sin, by delight we are conquered, by consent we are even bound.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Unde exclamandum nobis cum Apostolo est: Infelix ego homo, quis me liberabit de corpore mortis hujus? Ut audiamus subsequentem nos consolationem: Gratia Dei per Jesum Christum Dominum nostrum.
chunks.english: Hence we must cry out with the Apostle: Wretched man that I am, who will deliver me from this body of death? So that we may hear the consolation that follows: The grace of God through Jesus Christ our Lord. As Paul writes in his letter to the Romans, chapter seven, verses twenty-four and twenty-five.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque unum idemque peccatum poena est et causa peccati, quod melius ostendimus, si res ipsas ad medium deducimus. Effrenata enim ventris jugluvies in fervorem luxuriae plenitudinem carnis instigat, perpetrata autem luxuria, saepe aut perjurio aut homicidio tegitur, ne humanarum legum ultione puniatur.
chunks.english: Often one and the same sin is both punishment and cause of sin, which we show better if we bring the matters themselves to the middle. For unbridled gluttony of the belly incites the fullness of flesh to the heat of lust, but lust once committed is often covered by perjury or homicide, lest it be punished by the vengeance of human laws.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ponamus ante oculos, quod quidam voracitatis sibi frena laxavit, qua voracitate superatus adulterii facinus admisit: deprehensus autem in adulterio, latenter virum adulterae, ne ad judicium traheretur, occidit. Hoc itaque adulterium inter voracitatem et homicidium positum de illa videlicet nascens, hoc generans, peccatum est et poena et causa peccati.
chunks.english: Let us set before our eyes that someone loosened the reins of voracity for himself, by which voracity overcome he committed the crime of adultery: but caught in adultery, he secretly killed the adulterer's husband lest he be dragged to judgment. This adultery therefore, placed between voracity and homicide, arising from the former and generating the latter, is sin and punishment and cause of sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Peccatum profecto est per seipsum, poena vero peccati est, quia culpam voracitatis auxit. Causa autem peccati est, quia subsequens etiam homicidium genuit. Propheta Jeremias dicit: Visitabo super vos juxta fructum studiorum vestrorum. Peccatis aliis differunt peccata, quae per consilium perpetrantur.
chunks.english: It is certainly sin through itself, but it is punishment of sin because it increased the fault of voracity. It is cause of sin because it also generated the subsequent homicide. The prophet Jeremiah says: I will visit upon you according to the fruit of your endeavors. As Jeremiah, chapter twenty-one, verse fourteen says. Sins committed by deliberation differ from other sins.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Non enim tam prave facta Dominus quam studia pravitatis insequitur. In factis enim saepe infirmitate, saepe negligentia, in studiis vero semper malitiosa intentione peccatur. Beatus Job typum peccantium intra Ecclesiam designans, ait: Concidit me vulnere super vulnus.
chunks.english: For the Lord pursues not so much evil deeds as the zeal for wickedness. In deeds one often sins through weakness, often through negligence, but in deliberate efforts one always sins through malicious intention. Blessed Job, designating the type of those sinning within the Church, says: He has cut me down with wound upon wound. As the book of Job, chapter sixteen, verse fourteen records.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In infirmis suis sancta Ecclesia vulnere super vulnus conciditur, quando peccatum peccato additur, ut culpa vehementius exaggeretur. Quem enim avaritia pertrahit ad rapinam, rapina ducit ad fallaciam, ut perpetrata culpa ex falsitate etiam defendatur; quid iste, nisi super vulnus concisus est vulnere?
chunks.english: In her weak members holy Church is cut down with wound upon wound when sin is added to sin, so that the fault is more vehemently heaped up. For whom avarice drags to plunder, plunder leads to deceit, so that the committed fault is even defended by falsehood; what is this person but cut down with wound upon wound?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Bene per prophetam dicitur: Maledictum, mendacium, homicidium, furtum, et adulterium inundaverunt, et sanguis sanguinem tetigit. Sanguis ergo sanguinem tangit, cum culpa culpam cumulaverit. Cum igitur vulnus vulneri additur, vires contra nos antiqui hostis vehementius excitantur.
chunks.english: Well is it said through the prophet: Cursing, lying, murder, theft, and adultery have overflowed, and blood has touched blood. As the prophet Hosea, chapter four, verse two says. Blood therefore touches blood when fault has heaped up fault. When wound is added to wound, the forces of the ancient enemy are more vehemently aroused against us.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quamvis quolibet in loco cogitando, loquendo et agendo peccemus, tunc tamen per tria haec animus effrenatius rapitur, cum mundi hujus prosperitate sublevatur.
chunks.english: Although we sin anywhere by thinking, speaking, and acting, yet the mind is carried away more wildly through these three when it is lifted up by the prosperity of this world.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXVI. De manifestis, occultisque peccatis.
chunks.english: Chapter 36: Manifest and Hidden Sins

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Uniuscujusque hominis culpa latens, quasi vulva peccati est, quae occulte peccatorem concipit, et reatum suum in tenebris abscondit. A vulva enim de utero exitur, cum peccator quae in occulto commiserit haec etiam in aperto committere non erubescit. Scriptum quippe est: Egressus ex utero non statim perii? id est, postquam ad apertam iniquitatem exii, cur me tunc saltem perditum non cognovi?
chunks.english: Each person's hidden fault is like the womb of sin, which secretly conceives the sinner and hides his guilt in darkness. For one exits from the womb when the sinner is not ashamed to commit openly even those things he committed in secret. For it is written: Did I not perish immediately on leaving the womb? As the book of Job, chapter three, verse eleven says. That is, after I went out to open iniquity, why did I not at least then recognize myself as lost?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Periisset quippe in suo judicio, si se perditum cognovisset. Quasi ab occultationis suae vulva processerant, de quibus propheta dicebat: Peccatum suum sicut Sodoma praedicaverunt, nec absconderunt.
chunks.english: He would have perished in his own judgment if he had recognized himself as lost. They had come forth as if from the womb of their concealment, of whom the prophet said: They proclaimed their sin like Sodom, and did not hide it. As the prophet Isaiah, chapter three, verse nine records.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXVII. De his qui ad delicta post lacrymas revertuntur.
chunks.english: Chapter 37: Relapsing After Repentance

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sunt nonnulli qui quando ad mentem redeunt, Dei justitiam et rectitudinem contemplantur, et orando ac flendo contremiscunt; sed postquam contemplationis hora transierit, sic audaces ad iniquitatem redeunt, ac si post dorsum ejus positi a justitiae ejus lumine minime videantur.
chunks.english: There are some who when they come to their senses contemplate the justice and uprightness of God, and tremble while praying and weeping; but after the hour of contemplation has passed, they return so boldly to iniquity as if placed behind his back they are scarcely seen by the light of his justice.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quicunque post lacrymas ad delicta relabuntur, apud se in abscondito quasi corporalem videntem aspiciunt faciem Dei, quia ei, et cum praesentes fiunt, blandiuntur fletibus, et cum quasi a conspectu illius recedunt, moribus detrahunt. Qui tanto amplius de malis suis feriendi sunt, quanto et in occulto cogitationis recta Dei judicia cognoscunt.
chunks.english: Whoever relapses into sins after tears sees the face of God in secret within themselves as if seeing bodily, because they flatter him with tears when they come into his presence, and detract by their conduct when they withdraw as if from his sight. They are to be struck all the more for their evils because they know the righteous judgments of God even in the secret of thought.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sunt nonnulli qui post vitam perditam ad semetipsos redeunt, et accusante se conscientia, perversa itinera relinquunt, commutant opera, antiquae suae pravitati contradicunt, terrenas actiones fugiunt, desideria superna sectantur; sed priusquam in eisdem sanctis desideriis solidentur, per torporem mentis, ad ea quae dijudicare coeperant redeunt, atque ad mala quae fugere disposuerant recurrunt.
chunks.english: There are some who after a wasted life return to themselves, and with conscience accusing them, abandon perverse paths, change their works, contradict their former wickedness, flee earthly actions, pursue heavenly desires; but before they are solidified in these same holy desires, through torpor of mind they return to what they had begun to judge, and run back to the evils they had determined to flee.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Si quid boni fortasse homo agere coeperit, priusquam in eo per longitudinem temporis convalescat, ad exteriora relabitur, et perverse deserit quae recte inchoasse videbatur. Sunt nonnulli qui post perversa itinera sanctas vias sectari appetunt; sed priusquam in eis desideria bona roborentur, quaedam illos praesentis saeculi prosperitas accipit, quae eos rebus exterioribus implicat, et eorum mentem, dum a calore intimi amoris retrahit, quasi ex frigore exstinguit, et quidquid in eis de virtutum flore apparere videbatur interficit.
chunks.english: If perchance a person begins to do some good, before he grows strong in it through length of time, he relapses into external things and perversely abandons what he seemed to have rightly begun. There are some who after perverse paths desire to pursue holy ways; but before good desires are strengthened in them, some prosperity of the present age seizes them, which entangles them in external things, and while it withdraws their mind from the warmth of intimate love, extinguishes it as if by cold, and kills whatever seemed to appear in them of the flower of virtues.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Curandum nimis est, ne ad hoc quisque proruat, quod se mundasse fletibus exsultat, ne dum deplorata iterum culpa committitur, in conspectu justi Judicis ipsa etiam lamenta levigentur. Scriptum est in libro Ecclesiastici: Qui baptizatur a mortuo, et iterum tangit illum, quid proficit lavatio ejus?
chunks.english: Great care must be taken lest anyone fall back into that which he rejoices to have cleansed by tears, lest while the lamented fault is committed again, the very laments are made light in the sight of the just Judge. It is written in the book of Ecclesiasticus: He who is baptized from a dead person, and touches him again, what does his washing profit? As Ecclesiasticus, chapter thirty-four, verse thirty says.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Post lavacrum enim mundus esse negligit, quisquis post lacrymas vitae innocentiam non custodit; et lavantur ergo, et nequaquam mundi sunt, qui commissa flere non desinunt, sed rursum flenda committunt. Baptizatur scilicet a mortuo, qui mundatur fletibus a peccato; sed post baptisma mortuum tangit, qui culpam post lacrymas repetit.
chunks.english: For he neglects to be clean after washing, whoever does not guard the innocence of life after tears; and therefore they are washed and are in no way clean, who do not cease to weep for things committed, but again commit things to be wept over. He is baptized from a dead person who is cleansed by tears from sin; but he touches the dead after baptism who repeats the fault after tears.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXVIII. De peccandi consuetudine.
chunks.english: Chapter 38: The Habit of Sin

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Peccator, cum jam de iniquitate sua non confunditur, in iniquitate eadem etiam adminiculis pessimae consuetudinis roboratur. Quasi quibusdam oblectationibus peccator fovetur ut crescat, dum culpa consuetudinibus firmatur ut vigeat.
chunks.english: The sinner, when he is no longer ashamed of his iniquity, is strengthened in that same iniquity even by the supports of the worst habit. The sinner is fostered as if by certain delights so that he may grow, while the fault is strengthened by habits so that it may thrive.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cum prodire culpa in usum coeperit, nimirum se vel falsa spe divinae misericordiae, vel aperta miseria desperationis pascit, ut eo nequaquam ad correptionem quisque redeat, quo vel factorem suum pium sibi inordinate simulat, vel hoc quod fecit inordinate formidat.
chunks.english: When the fault begins to come forth into habit, it certainly feeds itself either with false hope of divine mercy or with the open misery of desperation, so that one in no way returns to correction, because either he imagines his maker as merciful to himself in a disordered way, or he fears in a disordered way what he has done.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Beatus Job humani generis lapsus aspicieus, et quibus praecipitiis mersum sit in foveam iniquitatis intendens, dicit: Quare non in vulva mortuus sum? id est, in ipsa occulta perpetratione peccati, cur a carnis vita mortificare me nolui? Quare, inquit, exceptus genibus, id est, etiam post apertam culpam, cur me adhuc in illa etiam consuetudo suscepit, ut valentiorem ad nequitias redderet, et pravis me usibus sustinens foveret?
chunks.english: Blessed Job, seeing the fall of the human race and considering into what precipices it is plunged in the pit of iniquity, says: Why did I not die in the womb? As the book of Job, chapter three, verse eleven says. That is, in the very hidden perpetration of sin, why did I not want to mortify myself from the life of the flesh? Why, he asks, was I received upon the knees? That is, even after open fault, why did habit still receive me in it, so as to make me stronger for wickedness, and fostered me by sustaining me in evil habits?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque cum culpa in usum venerit, ei jam animus etiam si appetat, debilius resistit, quia quot vicibus pravae frequentationis astringitur, quasi tot vinculis ad mentem ligatur. Nonnunquam fit ut enervis animus, cum solvi peccati consuetudine non valet, ad quaedam se solatia falsae consolationis inclinet, quatenus venturum Judicem tantae sibi misericordiae spondeat, ut eos etiam quos arguendos invenerit penitus non occidat.
chunks.english: Often when the fault has come into habit, the mind resists it more weakly even if it desires to, because by as many times as it is bound by evil repetition, by so many bonds is it bound to the mind. Sometimes it happens that the weak mind, when it cannot free itself from the habit of sin, inclines itself to certain solaces of false consolation, so that it promises itself such mercy from the coming Judge that he will not utterly destroy even those whom he finds deserving of rebuke.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Cui rei hoc deterius accidit, quod ei multorum similium lingua consentit, cum multi male gesta hominum laudibus exaggerant; unde fit plerumque ut incessanter crescat culpa favoribus enutrita. Curari autem vulnus negligitur, quod dignum praemio laudis videtur.
chunks.english: To this matter worse happens, that the tongue of many similar people consents to it, when many heap up the evil deeds of men with praises; whence it often happens that the fault grows incessantly nourished by favors. But the wound is neglected to be cured which seems worthy of the reward of praise.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Per Salomonem in Proverbiis dicitur; Fili mi, si te lactaverint peccatores, ne acquiescas. Peccatores enim lactant, cum vel perpetranda mala blandimentis inferunt, vel perpetrata favoribus extollunt. Isaias propheta ait: Vae qui trahitis in quitatem in funiculis vanitatis.
chunks.english: Through Solomon in Proverbs it is said: My son, if sinners entice you, do not consent. As Proverbs, chapter one, verse ten says. For sinners entice when they either bring in evils to be committed with blandishments, or exalt committed ones with favors. The prophet Isaiah says: Woe to those who drag iniquity with cords of vanity. As Isaiah, chapter five, verse eighteen says.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Iniquitas namque in funiculis vanitatis trahitur, dum per consuetudinis augmentum culpa protelatur. Beatus David de peccandi consuetudine dicit: Funes peccatorum circumplexi sunt me. Quia enim funis addendo torquetur, ut crescat, non immerito peccati consuetudo in fune figuratur, quod perverso corde, dum saepe frequentatur, multiplicius per augmentum consuetudinis augetur.
chunks.english: For iniquity is dragged with cords of vanity while the fault is prolonged through the increase of habit. Blessed David says of the habit of sinning: The cords of sinners have encompassed me. As the Psalm, one hundred eighteen, verse sixty-one says. Because a cord is twisted by adding to make it grow, not undeservedly is the habit of sin figured in a cord, which in a perverse heart, while often repeated, is increased more abundantly through the growth of habit.
chunks.notes: Citation: Psalm 118:61 (Vulgate numbering; = Psalm 119:61 in modern numbering)

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XXXIX. De levioribus peccatis.
chunks.english: Chapter 39: Lesser Sins

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Crebro peccatum, aut ignorantia, aut infirmitate perpetratur, ut vel nesciat homo quid velle debeat, vel non omne, quod voluerit, possit. Cum in peccato animus moritur, citius ad vitam reducitur, si super hoc sollicitae cogitationes vivunt.
chunks.english: Often sin is committed either by ignorance or by weakness, so that either a person does not know what he ought to will, or cannot do all that he has willed. When the mind dies in sin, it is more quickly brought back to life if careful thoughts live concerning this.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XL. De gravioribus peccatis.
chunks.english: Chapter 40: Graver Sins

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omne peccatum quod citius non tergitur, aut peccatum est et causa peccati, aut peccatum et poena peccati. Peccatum namque quod poenitentia non diluit ipso suo pondere mox ad aliud trahit. Unde fit ut non solum peccatum sit, sed peccatum et causa peccati. Ex illo quippe vitio culpa subsequens oritur, ex quo caeca mens ducitur, ut pejus ex alio ligetur.
chunks.english: Every sin that is not quickly wiped away is either sin and cause of sin, or sin and punishment of sin. For sin that repentance does not wash away immediately drags one to another by its very weight. Whence it happens that it is not only sin, but sin and cause of sin. For from that vice the subsequent fault arises, from which the blind mind is led so as to be more wickedly bound by another.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Peccatum, quod ex peccato oritur, non jam peccatum tantummodo, sed peccatum est et poena peccati, quia justo judicio omnipotens Deus cor peccantis obnubilat, ut praecedentis peccati merito etiam in aliis cadat. Quem enim liberare noluit, deserendo percussit. Non immerito poena peccati dicitur, quod justa desuper irrogata caecitate ex praecedentis peccati ultione perpetratur.
chunks.english: Sin that arises from sin is no longer merely sin, but is sin and punishment of sin, because by just judgment almighty God clouds the heart of the sinner, so that by the merit of the preceding sin he falls also in other things. For whom he did not want to free, he struck by abandoning. Not undeservedly is it called punishment of sin, which is committed by just blindness imposed from above as vengeance for the preceding sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Quod videlicet agitur dispositione superius ordinata, sed inferius iniquitate confusa, ut et praecedens culpa sit causa subsequentis; et rursum culpa subsequens sit poena praecedentis. Paulus apostolus in infidelibus et lubricis, quasi quoddam semen erroris aspexerat, cum dicebat: Qui cum cognovissent Deum, non sicut Deum glorificaverunt, aut gratias egerunt, sed evanuerunt in cogitationibus suis.
chunks.english: Which is done by disposition ordained from above, but confused by iniquity from below, so that both the preceding fault is cause of the subsequent; and again the subsequent fault is punishment of the preceding. Paul the apostle saw in the unfaithful and slippery as it were a certain seed of error, when he said: Who when they had known God, did not glorify him as God or give thanks, but became vain in their thoughts. As Paul writes in his letter to the Romans, chapter one, verse twenty-one.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ecce est peccatum et causa peccati, ex qua causa quid sequatur adjungit: Et obscuratum est insipiens cor eorum; dicentes enim se esse sapientes, stulti facti sunt; et mutaverunt gloriam incorruptibilis Dei in similitudinem imaginis corruptibilis hominis, et volucrum, et quadrupedum et serpentium. Ecce est peccatum et poena peccati.
chunks.english: Behold, this is sin and cause of sin, from which cause what follows he adds: And their foolish heart was darkened; for professing themselves to be wise, they became fools; and they changed the glory of the incorruptible God into the likeness of the image of corruptible man, and of birds, and of fourfooted beasts, and of creeping things. As Romans, chapter one, verses twenty-two and twenty-three say. Behold, this is sin and punishment of sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed peccatum solummodo et poena peccati esset, si non adhuc ex hoc peccato et aliud sequeretur. Nam post infidelitatem eorum subditur: Propter quod tradidit illos Deus in desideria cordis eorum, in immunditiam, ut contumeliis officiant corpora sua in semetipsis.
chunks.english: But it would be only sin and punishment of sin, if yet another did not still follow from this sin. For after their unfaithfulness it is added: Wherefore God delivered them up to the desires of their heart, unto uncleanness, to dishonor their own bodies among themselves. As Romans, chapter one, verse twenty-four says.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui cognoscentes Deum non sicut Deum glorificaverunt, ex eo peccato et causa peccati ad hoc quoque perducti sunt, ut ad cultum serpentium et volucrum laberentur. Sed quia per hanc etiam caecitatem usque ad immunditiam et carnis contumeliam ceciderunt, ipsa infidelitatis eorum caecitas praecedenti intellectui et peccatum est et poena peccati. Subsequenti vero immunditiae peccatum facta est et causa peccati.
chunks.english: Those who knowing God did not glorify him as God were led from that sin and cause of sin also to this, that they fell into the worship of serpents and birds. But because through this blindness also they fell all the way to uncleanness and bodily dishonor, the very blindness of their unfaithfulness is both sin and punishment of sin to the preceding understanding. But to the subsequent uncleanness it became sin and cause of sin.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Qui cognoscentes Deum peccatum superbiae intelligendo commiserunt, caecantur etiam, ne intelligant quod committunt. Et qui intelligentiam suam sequi nolunt in peccato et causa peccati, ipsum lumen intelligentiae perdunt in peccato et poena peccati. Prioris peccati merito peccatorum subsequentium fovea tegitur, ut qui malum sciens perpetrat, deinceps juste in aliis etiam nesciens cadat.
chunks.english: Those who knowing God committed the sin of pride by understanding are also blinded lest they understand what they commit. And those who are unwilling to follow their understanding in sin and cause of sin lose the very light of understanding in sin and punishment of sin. By the merit of the former sin the pit of subsequent sins is covered, so that he who knowingly perpetrates evil may thereafter justly fall also unknowingly in other things.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Hoc quippe agitur, ut culpis culpae feriantur, quatenus supplicia fiant peccantium ipsa incrementa vitiorum. Sunt multa peccata quae committimus, et idcirco gravia nobis non videntur, quia privato nos amore diligentes, clausis nobis oculis, in nostra deceptione blandimur.
chunks.english: For this is done, that faults may be struck by faults, so that the very increases of vices may become punishments of sinners. There are many sins that we commit, and therefore they do not seem grave to us, because loving ourselves with private love, with our eyes closed, we flatter ourselves in our own deception.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Plerumque fit ut et nostra gravia leviter, et proximorum mala levia graviter judicemus. Scriptum quippe est: Erunt homines seipsos amantes. Et scimus quia vehementer claudit oculum cordis amor privatus; ex quo fit ut hoc quod nos agimus, et grave esse non existimamus, plerumque agatur a proximo, et nimis nobis detestabile esse videatur.
chunks.english: It often happens that we judge our own grave sins lightly, and the light evils of our neighbors gravely. For it is written: Men will be lovers of themselves. As Paul writes in his second letter to Timothy, chapter three, verse two. And we know that private love strongly closes the eye of the heart; from which it happens that what we do, we do not consider grave, is often done by a neighbor, and seems too detestable to us.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Sed quare hoc quod nobis vile videbatur in nobis grave videtur in proximo, nisi quia nec nos sicut proximum, nec proximum conspicimus sicut nos? Si nos sicut proximum aspiceremus, nostra reprehensibilia districte videremus. Et rursum, si proximum aspiceremus ut nos, nunquam nobis ejus actio appareret intolerabilis, qui saepe fortasse talia egimus, et nihil nos proximo intolerabile fecisse putabamus.
chunks.english: But why does what seemed trivial to us in ourselves seem grave in a neighbor, except that we neither see ourselves as neighbor nor neighbor as ourselves? If we looked at ourselves as neighbor, we would see our own blameworthy things strictly. And again, if we looked at neighbor as ourselves, his action would never appear intolerable to us, since we have often perhaps done such things, and thought we had done nothing intolerable to our neighbor.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Male divisum mentis nostrae judicium corrigere per legis praeceptum Moyses studuit, cum dixit ut justus deberet esse modius aequusque sextarius. Hoc vero Salomon ait: Pondus et pondus, mensura et mensura, utrumque abominabile est apud Deum.
chunks.english: Moses strove to correct the wrongly divided judgment of our mind through the precept of the law, when he said that the measure should be just and the sextarius equal. As the book of Leviticus, chapter nineteen, verse thirty-six says. But Solomon says this: Weight and weight, measure and measure, both are abominable to God. As Proverbs, chapter twenty, verse ten says.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Scimus quia in negotiatorum duplici pondere aliud majus, aliud minus est. Nam aliud pondus habent, ad quod pensant sibi, aliud pondus ad quod pensant proximo. Ad dandum pondera leviora, ad accipiendum vero graviora, praeparant. Omnis homo qui aliter pensat ea quae proximi, et aliter ea quae sua sunt, pondus et pondus habet.
chunks.english: We know that in the double weight of merchants one is greater, another is less. For they have one weight by which they weigh for themselves, another weight by which they weigh for their neighbor. For giving they prepare lighter weights, but for receiving heavier ones. Every man who weighs the things of his neighbor one way and his own things another way has weight and weight.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Utrumque ergo abominabile est apud Deum, quia si sic proximum ut se diligeret, hunc in nobis sicut se amaret. Et si sic se sicut proximum aspiceret, se in malis sicut proximum judicaret.
chunks.english: Both therefore are abominable to God, because if he loved his neighbor as himself, he would love him in us as himself. And if he looked at himself as neighbor, he would judge himself in evils as neighbor.

chunks.speaker: announcer
chunks.latin: CAPUT XLI. De desperatione peccantium.
chunks.english: Chapter 41: The Desperation of Sinners

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Falsa spes aliquando mentem non intercipit, sed hanc deterior dispensatio configit; quae dum omnem spem veniae funditus interimit, erroris lacte animam uberius nutrit. Beatus Job desperantis uniuscujusque personam in sua locutione assumens, ait: Cur lactatus sum uberibus?
chunks.english: False hope sometimes does not cut off the mind, but a worse dispensation pierces it; which while it utterly destroys all hope of pardon, more abundantly nourishes the soul with the milk of error. Blessed Job, assuming in his speech the person of anyone who despairs, says: Why was I nursed at the breasts? As the book of Job, chapter three, verse twelve says.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Ac si dicat: Utinam blandiri mihi saltem post maia perpetrata noluissem, ne tanto me culpae nequius astringerem, quanto me in illa mollius foverem. Quisquis impietati succumbit, vitam profecto justitiae moriendo derelinquit. Qui vero etiam post peccatum mole desperationis obruitur, quid aliud quam post mortem in inferni supplicio sepelitur?
chunks.english: As if to say: Would that at least after committing evils I had not wanted to flatter myself, lest I bind myself the more wickedly to the fault the more gently I foster myself in it. Whoever succumbs to impiety certainly abandons the life of justice by dying. But who is also buried after sin under the weight of desperation—what else is he but buried after death in the torment of hell?

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: In libris Salomonis scriptum est: Impius, cum in profundum venerit peccatorum, contemnit. Redire namque dissimulat, quia misereri sibi posse desperat. Sed cum desperando amplius peccat, quasi puteo suo fundum subtrahit, ne ubi retineri possit inveniat.
chunks.english: In the books of Solomon it is written: The wicked man, when he has come into the depth of sins, despises. As Proverbs, chapter eighteen, verse three says. For he refuses to return, because he despairs that mercy can be shown to him. But when by despairing he sins more, he removes as it were the bottom from his well, lest he find a place where he can be held.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Omnis qui viam vitae deserens in peccatorum se tenebras dejicit, semetipsum quasi in puteum, vel in foveam, mergit; si vero diutina perpetratione etiam consuetudine iniquitatis opprimitur, ne ad superiora jam possit exsurgere, quasi angusto ore putei coarctatur.
chunks.english: Everyone who abandoning the way of life casts himself down into the darkness of sins plunges himself as into a well or a pit; but if by long perpetration he is also oppressed by the habit of iniquity so that he can no longer rise to higher things, he is constricted as by the narrow mouth of the well.

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: David propheta sub specie peccantium exorat, dicens: Non me demergat tempestas aquae, neque absorbeat me profundum, neque urgeat super me puteus os suum. Quem enim mali operis iniquitas a bona stabilitate commovit, quasi tempestas aquae rapuit. Sed si adhuc consuetudine non praevaluit, non demersit.
chunks.english: The prophet David prays under the guise of sinners, saying: Let not the tempest of water drown me, nor let the deep swallow me up, nor let the well close its mouth upon me. As the Psalm, sixty-eight, verse sixteen says. For whom the iniquity of evil work has moved from good stability, the tempest of water has seized. But if habit has not yet prevailed, it has not drowned him.
chunks.notes: Citation: Psalm 68:16 (Vulgate numbering; = Psalm 69:15 in modern numbering)

chunks.speaker: narrator
chunks.latin: Jam in puteum cecidit, qui hoc, quod divina lex prohibet perpetravit. Sed si adhuc longa consuetudo non deprimit, nequaquam os suum putens coangustavit. Tanto ergo facilius egreditur, quanto minori consuetudine coarctatur. Quasi quaedam conclusi oris angustia est, ab opprimente mala consuetudine exsurgere velle, nec posse; jam quidem desiderio ad superna tendere, sed adhuc actu in infimis remanere; praeire corde, nec tamen sequi opere, atque in semetipso contradictionem perpeti semetipsum.
chunks.english: He has already fallen into the well who has committed what divine law forbids. But if long habit does not yet weigh him down, the well has in no way narrowed its mouth. Therefore he exits more easily the less he is constricted by habit. It is like a certain narrowness of closed mouth to want to rise from oppressing evil habit and not be able; already indeed to tend to higher things by desire, but still to remain in the lowest by action; to go ahead in heart but not follow in work, and to suffer self-contradiction in himself.
